contentBlocks.title: Text 4: Documentary Review - "The Last Glaciers"
contentBlocks.content:
(1) "The Last Glaciers" is not just a documentary; it is a visceral wake-up call wrapped in stunning cinematography. Directed by Craig Leeson, the film takes audiences on a perilous journey to the world's disappearing ice caps. Unlike dry academic documentaries, this film uses the lens of extreme sports to convey its message. We watch athletes paragliding off peaks that are rapidly losing their snow cover, making the stakes feel physical and immediate. The visual contrast between the majestic white giants and the brown, barren earth left behind is heartbreaking.

(2) The narrative is compelling, weaving scientific data with personal stories of communities affected by glacial melt. However, the film is not without flaws. At times, the narration leans heavily into didactic territory, reiterating points that the visuals have already made clear. This repetition can feel slightly patronizing to an attentive audience. Despite this minor pacing issue, "The Last Glaciers" succeeds in making the abstract concept of climate change tangibly terrifying. It is essential viewing that demands to be seen on the biggest screen possible.

questions.explanation:
Paragraf terakhir menyimpulkan rekomendasi: "essential viewing... demands to be seen."
Answer: C

questions.question: What makes Wilma Rudolph's story in Text 5 particularly inspirational?

options.label: A
options.text: She was born into a wealthy family.

options.label: B
options.text: She overcame childhood paralysis to become the fastest woman in the world.

options.label: C
options.text: She invented a new style of running.

options.label: D
options.text: She won without ever practicing.

options.label: E
options.text: She retired early to become a doctor.

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 5: The Sprint of Wilma Rudolph
contentBlock.content:
(1) Wilma Rudolph was born prematurely in 1940, a sickly child in a poor family of 22 children. At age four, she contracted polio, which left her left leg twisted and paralyzed. Doctors said she would never walk without a brace. But Wilma and her mother refused to accept that verdict. For years, they traveled miles for treatments, and her brothers and sisters took turns massaging her leg. By age nine, she removed the metal brace. By age 11, she was playing basketball.

(2) Wilma didn't just want to walk; she wanted to run. She joined the track team, and although she lost every race initially, she never quit. Her relentless determination paid off. At the 1960 Rome Olympics, the girl who was told she would never walk became the first American woman to win three gold medals in a single Games. She was hailed as "The Black Gazelle." Wilma's story is a testament to the power of the human spirit to triumph over **adversity**. She leveled the playing field not by magic, but by pure grit, inspiring generations to believe that no hurdle is too high.

questions.question: What is the primary topic of Text 1?

options.label: A
options.text: The history of smartphone manufacturing.

options.label: B
options.text: The difference between resistive and capacitive touchscreens.

options.label: C
options.text: The working mechanism of capacitive touchscreens.

options.label: D
options.text: The decline of physical buttons in modern technology.

options.label: E
options.text: The electrical conductivity of the human body.

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 1: How Touchscreens Work
contentBlock.content:
(1) In the modern era, touchscreens are ubiquitous, found in everything from smartphones to ATM machines. While there are several types of touchscreens, the most common in consumer electronics is the **capacitive** touchscreen. Unlike the older resistive screens that relied on pressure to connect two conductive layers, capacitive screens work by sensing the electrical properties of the human body. Our bodies are naturally conductive; we carry a small electrical charge.

(2) A capacitive screen consists of a glass panel coated with a transparent conductor, such as indium tin oxide. When the screen is turned on, a small electrostatic field is created across the surface. When a finger touches the glass, it **distorts** the screen's electrostatic field. A tiny amount of charge is drawn to the point of contact, resulting in a voltage drop. Sensors located at the corners or within the grid of the screen detect this change in voltage and calculate the precise coordinates of the touch. This data is then sent to the device's processor to execute the command. This reliance on electrical conductivity explains why these screens do not work when wearing standard wool or cotton gloves, which act as insulators.

Answer: C

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 1: The Last Letter
contentBlock.content:
(1) Elias found the envelope tucked behind the drawer of the antique desk he had bought at the auction. The paper was yellowed and brittle, smelling of dust and time. The ink had **faded** into a ghostly gray, but the elegant handwriting remained legible. It was dated December 1899. Elias's hands trembled as he unfolded the letter. It was addressed to a woman named Clara from a soldier named Henry.

(2) "My dearest Clara," it began, "If you are reading this, it means I have not returned." The letter poured out a confession of love that Henry had been too afraid to speak in person. He spoke of the regret of silence, of the moments he watched her from afar, wishing for a courage that only came to him in the face of death. Elias read the words of a ghost, feeling the weight of a century-old heartbreak. He realized then how fragile life was, and how heavy the words left unspoken could become. He put the letter down and picked up his phone. He dialed the number he had been too stubborn to call for years. "Hello, Dad?" he said, his voice cracking. "I just wanted to talk."

questions.question: What is the main idea of Text 2 regarding photosynthesis?

options.label: A
options.text: Photosynthesis is a chemical process used only by algae.

options.label: B
options.text: The process of photosynthesis converts light energy into chemical energy, sustaining life on Earth.

options.label: C
options.text: Photosynthesis releases carbon dioxide into the atmosphere.

options.label: D
options.text: Plants need sugar to produce sunlight.

options.label: E
options.text: The Calvin Cycle happens at night.

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 2: The Engine of Life: Photosynthesis
contentBlock.content:
(1) Photosynthesis is the biological process by which green plants, algae, and some bacteria convert light energy into chemical energy. This process is the foundation of life on Earth, as it provides the primary energy source for nearly all ecosystems. It occurs mainly in the leaves of plants, specifically within organelles called chloroplasts, which contain the green pigment chlorophyll.

(2) The process can be divided into two main stages: the light-dependent reactions and the Calvin cycle. In the light-dependent reactions, sunlight hits the chlorophyll, exciting electrons and splitting water molecules into oxygen, hydrogen, and electrons. The oxygen is released into the atmosphere as a byproduct—a crucial one for aerobic life. The energy generated is then used in the Calvin cycle, which takes place in the stroma of the chloroplast. Here, carbon dioxide from the air is converted into glucose (sugar) using the energy stored from the first stage. This glucose serves as the building block for plant growth and provides food for other organisms in the food web.

(2) The process can be divided into two main stages: the light-dependent reactions and the Calvin cycle. In the light-dependent reactions, sunlight hits the chlorophyll, exciting electrons and splitting water molecules into oxygen, hydrogen, and electrons. The oxygen is released into the atmosphere as a byproduct—a crucial one for aerobic life. The energy generated is then used in the Calvin cycle, which takes place in the stroma of the chloroplast. Here, carbon dioxide from the air is converted into glucose (sugar) using the energy stored from the first stage. This glucose serves as the building block for plant growth and provides food for other organisms in the food web.

contentBlock.title: Text 3: The Shift to Remote Work
contentBlock.content:
(1) The traditional 9-to-5 office model is undergoing a seismic shift. The rise of remote work, accelerated by global events and technological advancements, has sparked a debate about the future of employment. Proponents argue that remote work offers unparalleled flexibility, allowing employees to balance their professional and personal lives more effectively. By eliminating the daily commute, workers save time and reduce stress, which studies have shown leads to increased productivity and job satisfaction.

(2) Furthermore, remote work benefits employers by reducing overhead costs associated with maintaining large office spaces. It also opens up the talent pool, allowing companies to hire the best candidates regardless of their geographical location. While critics point to potential challenges in communication and team cohesion, these can be mitigated with the right digital tools and management strategies. Ultimately, the shift to remote or hybrid work models represents a win-win solution that prioritizes efficiency and well-being. Companies that refuse to adapt may find themselves left behind in the race for talent.

contentBlock.title: Text 4: Book Review - "The Silent Ocean"
contentBlock.content:
(1) "The Silent Ocean" by J.K. Thorne is a hauntingly beautiful exploration of isolation and discovery. Set in a futuristic underwater colony, the novel combines hard science fiction with a deeply personal mystery. Thorne's prose is lyrical and atmospheric; he creates an **immersive** world where the pressure of the deep sea is palpable on every page. The protagonist, Dr. Aris, is a deeply complex character whose internal struggles mirror the turbulence of the ocean outside.

(2) The narrative is a slow burn. Readers expecting fast-paced action might find the first half of the book testing their patience. Thorne takes his time building the world and the tension. However, the middle section suffers from pacing issues, where some descriptions of the marine biology felt repetitive and slowed the plot unnecessarily. Despite this, the payoff in the final chapters is worth every page. The conclusion is emotional and thought-provoking, leaving the reader with questions about humanity's place in the universe. It is a imperfect but memorable journey.

questions.question: What is the primary inspiration derived from the story of inventor Sarah?

options.label: A
options.text: Failure is a sign to stop trying.

options.label: B
options.text: Innovation requires expensive equipment.

options.label: C
options.text: Resilience and viewing failure as data can lead to breakthrough success.

options.label: D
options.text: Inventions are only for geniuses.

options.label: E
options.text: You should always listen to critics.

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 5: The Light in the Bulb
contentBlock.content:
(1) Sarah was not a born genius, but she was stubborn. She had an idea for a solar-powered lamp that could be made from recycled plastic, affordable for the poorest villages. Prototype 1 failed. The circuit blew up. Prototype 50 failed. The plastic melted. People told her to get a real job. They said she was wasting her time on trash. But Sarah saw failure differently. "Failure is just data," she would say. "It tells me what doesn't work."

(2) She spent nights awake, tweaking wires and testing batteries. Prototype 101 flickered and died. Prototype 500 worked for an hour. Finally, Prototype 874 stayed lit. It shone with a steady, bright light. When she brought her lamps to a village without electricity, she saw children reading books after sunset for the first time. The smiles on their faces were brighter than her invention. Sarah's persistence hadn't just created a lamp; it had illuminated the world, literally and figuratively, proving that the brightest ideas often come from the darkest moments of struggle.

It shone with a steady, bright light. When she brought her lamps to a village without electricity, she saw children reading books after sunset for the first time. The smiles on their faces were brighter than her invention. Sarah's persistence hadn't just created a lamp; it had illuminated the world, literally and figuratively, proving that the brightest ideas often come from the darkest moments of struggle.

contentBlocks.content:

"Failure is just data," she would say. "It tells me what doesn't work."

It shone with a steady, bright light. When she brought her lamps to a village without electricity, she saw children reading books after sunset for the first time. Teks menyebut: "Prototype 101 failed... Prototype 500 failed... But with each failure, she tweaked the design." Ini menyimpulkan sukses karena iterasi yang gigih (*persistent iteration*).
Answer: C

questions.question: What is the central theme of the story "The Last Letter"?

options.label: A
options.text: The importance of writing skills in the 19th century.

options.label: B
options.text: The regret of lost opportunities and the enduring power of love.

options.label: C
options.text: A historical account of the war.

options.label: D
options.text: The difficulty of sending mail during winter.

options.label: E
options.text: A mystery about a stolen inheritance.

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 1: The Last Letter
contentBlock.content:
(1) Elias found the envelope tucked behind the drawer of the antique desk he had bought at the auction. The paper was yellowed and brittle, smelling of dust and time. The ink had **faded** into a ghostly gray, but the elegant handwriting remained legible. It was dated December 1899. Elias's hands trembled as he unfolded the letter. It was addressed to a woman named Clara from a soldier named Henry.

(2) "My dearest Clara," it began, "If you are reading this, it means I have not returned." The letter poured out a confession of love that Henry had been too afraid to speak in person. He spoke of the regret of silence, of the moments he watched her from afar, wishing for a courage that only came to him in the face of death. Elias read the words of a ghost, feeling the weight of a century-old heartbreak. He realized then how fragile life was, and how heavy the words left unspoken could become. He put the letter down and picked up his phone. He dialed the number he had been too stubborn to call for years. "Hello, Dad?" he said, his voice cracking. "I just wanted to talk."

questions.explanation:

Answer: B

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 1: The Psychology of Doomscrolling
contentBlock.content:
(1) Have you ever found yourself lying in bed at 2 AM, endlessly swiping through bad news on your phone, unable to look away despite feeling increasingly anxious? This phenomenon is known as "doomscrolling." Psychologists suggest that this behavior is rooted in the human brain's evolutionary design. Our brains are wired for **vigilance**, constantly scanning for potential threats to ensure survival. In the digital age, this instinct is hijacked by the endless stream of alarming information available at our fingertips.

(2) Evolutionarily, this behavior made sense; for our ancestors, paying attention to bad news (like a predator nearby) was a matter of life and death. However, in the modern context, algorithms are designed to keep us engaged by feeding this instinct, presenting a never-ending buffet of crises. This creates a feedback loop: the more we scroll, the more the brain perceives danger, triggering the release of stress hormones like cortisol. Instead of finding a solution or safety, the user is trapped in a cycle of anxiety, reinforcing the feeling that the world is unsafe while simultaneously disrupting sleep and mental well-being.

questions.subject: Literasi
questions.subcategory: Gagasan Pendirian yang Relevan/Tidak Relevan dengan Isi Bacaan Argumentatif

contentBlock.title: Text 2: Gamification in Education
contentBlock.content:
(1) Traditional education systems often struggle with student engagement, leading to boredom and a lack of motivation. A promising solution to this age-old problem is "gamification"—the integration of game mechanics into non-game environments. By incorporating elements such as points, badges, leaderboards, and immediate feedback, educators can transform passive learning into an active, dynamic experience. This approach taps into innate psychological needs for competence, autonomy, and relatedness, making the learning process not just a duty, but a compelling pursuit.

(2) Critics argue that gamification might oversimplify complex subjects or create an unhealthy competitive environment. However, when implemented correctly, the benefits outweigh the risks. Gamification provides a safe space for students to fail. In a game, losing a life is not a permanent failure but a chance to try again; similarly, in a gamified classroom, failure is just a stepping stone to mastery. This encourages resilience and a growth mindset. Furthermore, the instant gratification of unlocking a new level or badge releases dopamine, reinforcing positive learning behaviors and keeping students motivated to persist through difficult material.

- B (Benar): "It turns passive listening into active participation" — mengubah pelajar dari penerima pasif menjadi peserta aktif.
- C (Salah): Bertentangan dengan argumen positif penulis; penulis menunjukkan manfaat, bukan kerugian.
- D (Benar): "It provides a safe environment to fail and try again (resilience)" — teks secara eksplisit menyatakan: "In a game, losing a life is not a permanent failure but a chance to try again; similarly, in a gamified classroom, failure is just a stepping stone to mastery. This encourages resilience."
- E (Salah): "Increases the cost of textbooks significantly" — Tidak dibahas dalam teks sama sekali.

Jawaban: A, B, D

questions.question: The phrase "failure is just a stepping stone" in Text 2 implies that gamification teaches students to...

options.label: A
options.text: Avoid failure at all costs.

options.label: B
options.text: View mistakes as opportunities for growth and learning.

options.label: C
options.text: Ignore their grades completely.

options.label: D
options.text: Cheat to win the game.

options.label: E
options.text: Give up when the level gets hard.

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 2: Gamification in Education
contentBlock.content:
(1) Traditional education systems often struggle with student engagement, leading to boredom and a lack of motivation. A promising solution to this age-old problem is "gamification"—the integration of game mechanics into non-game environments. By incorporating elements such as points, badges, leaderboards, and immediate feedback, educators can transform passive learning into an active, dynamic experience. This approach taps into innate psychological needs for competence, autonomy, and relatedness, making the learning process not just a duty, but a compelling pursuit.

(2) Critics argue that gamification might oversimplify complex subjects or create an unhealthy competitive environment. However, when implemented correctly, the benefits outweigh the risks. Gamification provides a safe space for students to fail. In a game, losing a life is not a permanent failure but a chance to try again; similarly, in a gamified classroom, failure is just a stepping stone to mastery. This encourages resilience and a growth mindset. Furthermore, the instant gratification of unlocking a new level or badge releases dopamine, reinforcing positive learning behaviors and keeping students motivated to persist through difficult material.

questions.question: In the story "The Clockmaker's Secret" (Text 3), what does the "Grand Clock" symbolize for the townspeople?

options.label: A
options.text: A simple tool to tell time.

options.label: B
options.text: The tyranny of the King.

options.label: C
options.text: The heartbeat of the town and the order of their lives.

options.label: D
options.text: A waste of taxpayer money.

options.label: E
options.text: The magical power of the Clockmaker.

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 3: The Clockmaker's Secret
contentBlock.content:
(1) In the town of Oakhaven, time was not measured by the sun, but by the Grand Clock in the town square. Elias, the old clockmaker, was the only one who understood its intricate gears. He tended to it like a gardener tends to a rare orchid. For decades, the clock's rhythmic *tick-tock* was the heartbeat of the town, governing when shops opened, when children went to school, and when the bakers pulled bread from their ovens. The townspeople took it for granted, assuming the clock would beat forever.

(2) One winter morning, the clock stopped. Silence fell over Oakhaven, heavier than the snow. Without the chimes, the town fell into a **discordant** rhythm. Bakers burnt their bread, students were late, and arguments broke out in the confusion. Elias, frail and bedridden with age, heard the silence. He knew the main spring had finally snapped. Despite his aching bones, he forced himself up. He climbed the icy tower steps, his breath forming clouds in the freezing air. It wasn't just a machine to him; it was the order of his world. He worked through the night, his trembling hands finding strength in purpose. When the morning chime finally rang out, the town cheered, unaware that the clockmaker had given his last breath to restart their time.

questions.explanation:
Jam Besar (*Grand Clock*) dijelaskan sebagai pusat kehidupan kota, mengatur ritme mereka. Ketika jam berhenti, kota menjadi kacau. Ini menyimbolkan *order* (keteraturan) dan *heartbeat* kehidupan kota.
Answer: C

questions.subject: Literasi
questions.subcategory: Ketepatan Opini atas Objek Bahasan dalam Bacaan Ulasan

contentBlock.title: Text 4: Restaurant Review - "The Green Leaf"
contentBlock.content:
(1) "The Green Leaf," the city's newest vegetarian bistro, promises an innovative take on plant-based cuisine, and for the most part, it delivers. The menu is a testament to creativity, transforming humble vegetables into gastronomic stars. The "Beetroot Carpaccio" is a standout, plated with the precision of a painter. Visually, the food is a feast for the eyes, vibrant and inviting. The chef's commitment to farm-to-table freshness is evident in every bite, offering flavors that are robust and natural.

(2) However, the dining experience is somewhat marred by the environment. The restaurant aims for an "industrial-chic" vibe, but ends up feeling cold and echoing. The acoustics leave much to be desired. The minimalist concrete walls reflect every laugh and clatter of cutlery, creating a din that makes conversation difficult without raising one's voice. For a place that serves such delicate food, the atmosphere is surprisingly chaotic. While "The Green Leaf" succeeds in the kitchen, it fails to provide the relaxing sanctuary that its name suggests.

questions.question: In Text 5 (The Story of Aran), what is the primary source of inspiration derived from Aran's journey?

options.label: A
options.text: Winning a lottery is the key to happiness.

options.label: B
options.text: Environmental conservation can revitalize a community and restore nature.

options.label: C
options.text: Moving to the city is better than staying in a village.

options.label: D
options.text: Planting trees is a waste of time.

options.label: E
options.text: Droughts are unavoidable and cannot be fixed.

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 5: The Story of Aran
contentBlock.content:
(1) Aran lived in a village that was slowly being swallowed by the desert. The river had dried up years ago, and the young people had all left for the city. But Aran stayed. Every day, he walked to the barren hills with a sack of seeds and a heavy pot of water on his back. Many called him a madman, wasting water on dead soil. "The earth is sleeping," Aran would say, "not dead." He dug holes in the hard, cracked ground and planted saplings—neem, banyan, and peepal.

(2) Years turned into decades. Aran's back grew bent, and his hair turned white. But the hills changed too. The saplings became a forest. The roots held the rainwater, recharging the groundwater table. One monsoon, a miracle happened: the dry riverbed began to flow again. Birds returned, bringing more seeds. The village wells filled up. The people who had left began to return, finding their dusty home turned into an oasis. Aran, the madman, had sowed seeds of hope in the dust, and in doing so, he had breathed life back into his dying world.

Answer: B

contentBlocks.title: Text 5: The Story of Aran
contentBlocks.content:
(1) Aran lived in a village that was slowly being swallowed by the desert. The river had dried up years ago, and the young people had all left for the city. But Aran stayed. Every day, he walked to the barren hills with a sack of seeds and a heavy pot of water on his back. Many called him a madman, wasting water on dead soil. "The earth is sleeping," Aran would say, "not dead." He dug holes in the hard, cracked ground and planted saplings—neem, banyan, and peepal.

(2) Years turned into decades. Aran's back grew bent, and his hair turned white. But the hills changed too. The saplings became a forest. The roots held the rainwater, recharging the groundwater table. One monsoon, a miracle happened: the dry riverbed began to flow again. Birds returned, bringing more seeds. The village wells filled up. The people who had left began to return, finding their dusty home turned into an oasis. Aran, the madman, had sowed seeds of hope in the dust, and in doing so, he had breathed life back into his dying world.

questions.explanation:
Teks menyebut: "Many called him a madman, wasting water on dead soil." Ini menunjukkan reaksi skeptis dan meremehkan (*dismissive*).
Answer: C

options.label: C
options.text: Aran's decision to build a factory in the forest.

options.label: D
options.text: The long duration (decades) of his solitary effort.

options.label: E
options.text: The government's immediate intervention.

questions.question: What is the main idea of Text 1?

options.label: A
options.text: The psychological effects of doomscrolling on teenagers.

options.label: B
options.text: Doomscrolling as a compulsive behavior driven by the brain's survival mechanism.

options.label: C
options.text: The history of social media algorithms and their impact on sleep.

options.label: D
options.text: How to stop using smartphones at night effectively.

options.label: E
options.text: The financial benefits of social media platforms for news outlets.

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 1: The Psychology of Doomscrolling
contentBlock.content:
(1) Have you ever found yourself lying in bed at 2 AM, endlessly swiping through bad news on your phone, unable to look away despite feeling increasingly anxious? This phenomenon is known as "doomscrolling." Psychologists suggest that this behavior is rooted in the human brain's evolutionary design. Our brains are wired for **vigilance**, constantly scanning for potential threats to ensure survival. In the digital age, this instinct is hijacked by the endless stream of alarming information available at our fingertips.

(2) Evolutionarily, this behavior made sense; for our ancestors, paying attention to bad news (like a predator nearby) was a matter of life and death. However, in the modern context, algorithms are designed to keep us engaged by feeding this instinct, presenting a never-ending buffet of crises. This creates a feedback loop: the more we scroll, the more the brain perceives danger, triggering the release of stress hormones like cortisol. Instead of finding a solution or safety, the user is trapped in a cycle of anxiety, reinforcing the feeling that the world is unsafe while simultaneously disrupting sleep and mental well-being.

questions.explanation:
Teks 1 membahas definisi *doomscrolling*, mekanisme evolusi otak yang menyebabkannya (mencari ancaman/survival), dan siklus kecemasan yang ditimbulkannya. Opsi B paling tepat merangkum inti bacaan.
Answer: B

options.label: B
options.text: To ignore

options.label: C
options.text: To alleviate

options.label: D
options.text: To observe

options.label: E

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 1: The Rise of Vertical Farming
contentBlock.content:
(1) As the global population continues to skyrocket towards a projected 9 billion by 2050, the demand for food is increasing rapidly. However, arable land is becoming scarce due to urbanization and climate change. Enter vertical farming: the practice of growing crops in vertically stacked layers. It often incorporates controlled-environment agriculture, which aims to optimize plant growth, and soilless farming techniques such as hydroponics, aquaponics, and aeroponics. This modern method helps to mitigate the environmental impact of agriculture by reducing the need for land and water.

(2) In a typical vertical farm, plants are grown indoors under artificial conditions. LED lighting replaces sunlight, providing the specific light spectrums plants need for photosynthesis. This allows farmers to grow crops year-round, regardless of weather conditions outside. Furthermore, the hydroponic system delivers nutrients directly to the roots via a water solvent, a process that recycles water and uses up to 95% less water than traditional farming. Because the environment is tightly controlled, there is no need for pesticides, making the produce cleaner. Moreover, vertical farms can be built in the heart of cities, drastically reducing "food miles"—the distance food travels from farm to fork—thereby lowering transportation emissions.

questions.explanation:
Konteks kalimat: "This method helps to **mitigate** the environmental impact..." (*Metode ini membantu ... dampak lingkungan*). Kata yang paling tepat adalah *to alleviate* (meringankan/mengurangi). *Worsen* adalah antonim.
Answer: C

questions.explanation:
- A (Benar): Teks menyebutkan "year-round crop production" (bebas musim).
- B (Benar): Teks menyebutkan penggunaan "vertical stacks" menghemat lahan.
- C (Salah): Teks menyebutkan tantangan biaya energi dan *setup* yang tinggi.
- D (Benar): Teks menyebutkan lokasi di area urban mengurangi jarak transportasi (*food miles*).
- E (Salah): Tidak ada klaim "bebas semua bakteri", hanya pengurangan pestisida.
Answer: A, B, D

questions.question: What is the author's primary stance regarding the implementation of Universal Basic Income (UBI)?

options.label: A
options.text: The author strictly opposes UBI due to its potential to cause inflation.

options.label: B
options.text: The author supports UBI as a necessary evolution of the social safety net in the age of automation.

options.label: C
options.text: The author remains neutral and only presents statistical data without an opinion.

options.label: D
options.text: The author believes UBI should only be given to the elderly.

options.label: E
options.text: The author argues that UBI will make people lazy and stop working.

questions.subject: Literasi
questions.subcategory: Gagasan Pendirian yang Relevan/Tidak Relevan dengan Isi Bacaan Argumentatif

contentBlock.title: Text 2: The Case for Universal Basic Income
contentBlock.content:
(1) The rapid advancement of artificial intelligence and automation poses a unique threat to the global workforce. As algorithms and robots become more capable, millions of low-skilled and even some high-skilled jobs are at risk of obsolescence. To prevent widespread poverty and social unrest, governments must consider implementing a Universal Basic Income (UBI). UBI is a system where all citizens receive a set amount of money from the government regularly, regardless of their income or employment status. This financial floor would provide a safety net, ensuring that everyone can meet their basic needs in an unpredictable economy.

(2) Critics often argue that free money will incentivize laziness and discourage work. However, evidence from pilot programs in countries like Finland and Canada suggests otherwise. Participants in these programs did not drop out of the workforce; instead, many used the financial stability to pursue education, start businesses, or care for family members. UBI does not replace the dignity of work but empowers individuals to find work that is meaningful rather than working solely for survival. Furthermore, by placing money directly into the hands of consumers, UBI would stimulate the economy and support small businesses. It is a necessary evolution of the social contract in the digital age.

Answer: B

contentBlock.content:
(1) The rapid advancement of artificial intelligence and automation poses a unique threat to the global workforce. As algorithms and robots become more capable, millions of low-skilled and even some high-skilled jobs are at risk of obsolescence. To prevent widespread poverty and social unrest, governments must consider implementing a Universal Basic Income (UBI). UBI is a system where all citizens receive a set amount of money from the government regularly, regardless of their income or employment status. This financial floor would provide a safety net, ensuring that everyone can meet their basic needs in an unpredictable economy.

(2) Critics often argue that free money will incentivize laziness and discourage work. However, evidence from pilot programs in countries like Finland and Canada suggests otherwise. Participants in these programs did not drop out of the workforce; instead, many used the financial stability to pursue education, start businesses, or care for family members. UBI does not replace the dignity of work but empowers individuals to find work that is meaningful rather than working solely for survival. Furthermore, by placing money directly into the hands of consumers, UBI would stimulate the economy and support small businesses. It is a necessary evolution of the social contract in the digital age.

questions.explanation:

- B (Benar): Fakta pendukung dari program percontohan (*pilot programs*).
- C (Salah): Teks justru membantah ini.
- D (Benar): Kritik terhadap sistem lama mendukung perlunya sistem baru (UBI).
- E (Salah): Belum ada implementasi nasional di seluruh G20.
Answer: A, B, D

questions.question: In the story "The Lantern Maker," what does the lantern symbolize for the protagonist, Mei?

options.label: A
options.text: The financial burden of her family.

options.label: B
options.text: Her desire to travel the world.

options.label: C
options.text: A beacon of hope and the continuation of her grandfather's legacy.

options.label: D
options.text: The darkness of her past.

options.label: E
options.text: A tool to burn down the old village.

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 3: The Lantern Maker
contentBlock.content:
(1) The old shop smelled of bamboo shavings and rice glue. Mei sat cross-legged on the floor, her calloused fingers deftly bending a strip of bamboo into a perfect circle. Outside, the neon lights of the new shopping district flickered, casting artificial shadows that danced like ghosts against her paper windows. The encroaching shadows of the skyscrapers threatened to swallow her tiny shop, a relic of a bygone era in the middle of a modern metropolis. Developers had come yesterday, offering a suitcase full of cash for the land. "Think of your future," they had said.

(2) Mei looked at the unfinished lantern in her hands. It was a red phoenix, the symbol of rebirth. Her grandfather had taught her that a lantern was not just a light; it was a guide for wandering souls. If she sold the shop, the art would die with her. The money could buy her a comfortable apartment in one of those glass towers, but it could not buy the warmth of the wood or the stories painted on silk. She picked up her brush and dipped it into the ink. She would not sell. She would finish the phoenix. Let the skyscrapers rise; her light would shine small, but it would be true.

Answer: C

options.label: B
options.text: It was visually stunning but distracted from the plot.

options.label: C
options.text: It was the only redeeming quality of the film.

options.label: D
options.text: It was innovative and effectively set the mood.

options.label: E
options.text: It was copied from other sci-fi movies.

questions.subject: Literasi
questions.subcategory: Ketepatan Opini atas Objek Bahasan dalam Bacaan Ulasan

contentBlock.title: Text 4: Movie Review - "Blue Galaxy"
contentBlock.content:
(1) "Blue Galaxy," the latest sci-fi offering from director Elena Vance, is a visual masterpiece that unfortunately forgets to tell a compelling story. The visual language of "Blue Galaxy" is nothing short of breathtaking. The cinematography utilizes a cold, isolated palette that effectively immerses the audience in the film's melancholic tone. The seamless integration of visual effects (CGI) creates a believable futuristic world that feels lived-in rather than shiny and fake. The sound design also deserves praise; the auditory experience is visceral, with the silence of space used as a powerful narrative tool.

(2) However, the film drags significantly in the second act, where the narrative momentum seems to stall in favor of long, contemplative shots of stars. While beautiful, these scenes do not advance the plot or deepen our understanding of the conflict. The character development of the protagonist, Captain Haze, starts strong but fizzles out, leaving us with an emotional climax that feels unearned. While "Blue Galaxy" triumphs as a sensory experience, it fails to connect on a human level, making it a beautiful shell with a hollow center.

questions.question: What is the primary inspiration conveyed in the story of athlete Maya?

options.label: A
options.text: Winning is the only thing that matters.

options.label: B
options.text: Physical disability is a barrier that cannot be overcome.

options.label: C
options.text: Perseverance and adaptability can turn tragedy into triumph.

options.label: D
options.text: Sports are dangerous and should be avoided.

options.label: E
options.text: One should always rely on others for help.

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 5: Unstoppable Maya
contentBlock.content:
(1) Maya was born to run. By the age of 18, she was the state champion in track and field, with scholarships lining up at her door. Then came the accident. A drunk driver swerved onto the sidewalk, changing Maya's life in a split second. She woke up in the hospital to the news that she had lost her left leg below the knee. For months, Maya stared at the ceiling, thinking her life was over. The track was a distant memory, replaced by the cold reality of a prosthetic limb and physical therapy sessions that felt like torture.

(2) But the spirit of an athlete is not easily broken. One day, she saw a video of a sprinter with a carbon-fiber blade. A spark ignited. Maya returned to the track, not to run away from her pain, but to run through it. She fell a thousand times, bruised and exhausted, but she got up a thousand and one times. Three years later, Maya didn't just walk; she flew. She qualified for the Paralympics, breaking the national record. She forged a new path, proving that while she might have lost a limb, she had gained wings. Today, she mentors young athletes with disabilities, teaching them that the finish line is just the beginning.

Answer: C

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 5: Unstoppable Maya
contentBlock.content:
(1) Maya was born to run. By the age of 18, she was the state champion in track and field, with scholarships lining up at her door. Then came the accident. A drunk driver swerved onto the sidewalk, changing Maya's life in a split second. She woke up in the hospital to the news that she had lost her left leg below the knee. For months, Maya stared at the ceiling, thinking her life was over. The track was a distant memory, replaced by the cold reality of a prosthetic limb and physical therapy sessions that felt like torture.

(2) But the spirit of an athlete is not easily broken. One day, she saw a video of a sprinter with a carbon-fiber blade. A spark ignited. Maya returned to the track, not to run away from her pain, but to run through it. She fell a thousand times, bruised and exhausted, but she got up a thousand and one times. Three years later, Maya didn't just walk; she flew. She qualified for the Paralympics, breaking the national record. She forged a new path, proving that while she might have lost a limb, she had gained wings. Today, she mentors young athletes with disabilities, teaching them that the finish line is just the beginning.

Answer: B

questions.question: What is the main idea of the passage?

options.label: A
options.text: The challenges of implementing vertical farming in rural areas.

options.label: B
options.text: Vertical farming as a sustainable solution to food security and urbanization issues.

options.label: C
options.text: The history of agriculture from traditional methods to modern technology.

options.label: D
options.text: The economic disadvantages of using LED lights in farming.

options.label: E
options.text: The nutritional differences between vertically grown crops and traditional crops.

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 1: The Rise of Vertical Farming
contentBlock.content:
(1) As the global population continues to skyrocket towards a projected 9 billion by 2050, the demand for food is increasing rapidly. However, arable land is becoming scarce due to urbanization and climate change. Enter vertical farming: the practice of growing crops in vertically stacked layers. It often incorporates controlled-environment agriculture, which aims to optimize plant growth, and soilless farming techniques such as hydroponics, aquaponics, and aeroponics. This modern method helps to mitigate the environmental impact of agriculture by reducing the need for land and water.

(2) In a typical vertical farm, plants are grown indoors under artificial conditions. LED lighting replaces sunlight, providing the specific light spectrums plants need for photosynthesis. This allows farmers to grow crops year-round, regardless of weather conditions outside. Furthermore, the hydroponic system delivers nutrients directly to the roots via a water solvent, a process that recycles water and uses up to 95% less water than traditional farming. Because the environment is tightly controlled, there is no need for pesticides, making the produce cleaner. Moreover, vertical farms can be built in the heart of cities, drastically reducing "food miles"—the distance food travels from farm to fork—thereby lowering transportation emissions.

questions.explanation:
Teks 1 membahas konsep *vertical farming*, keuntungannya (hemat lahan, air, panen sepanjang tahun), dan potensinya sebagai solusi masa depan untuk ketahanan pangan di tengah urbanisasi. Opsi B merangkum inti bacaan ini dengan tepat.
Answer: B

questions.question:
(Select the correct answers - there can be more than one)
Based on Text 1, which statements accurately describe the potential benefits of cultured meat mentioned in the text?

options.label: A
options.text: It eliminates the need to slaughter animals for food.

options.label: B
options.text: It significantly reduces the land and water usage required for production compared to livestock farming.

options.label: C
options.text: It allows for the production of meat without the risk of fecal contamination or antibiotics.

options.label: D
options.text: It is currently cheaper to produce than conventional meat.

options.label: E
options.text: It tastes exactly like chicken regardless of the cells used.

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 1: The Cultured Meat Revolution
contentBlock.content:
(1) For centuries, eating meat meant raising, feeding, and slaughtering a whole animal. This process is resource-intensive: livestock farming occupies nearly 80% of global agricultural land and is a major source of methane, a potent greenhouse gas. Enter "cultured meat," also known as lab-grown or cultivated meat. This technology promises to produce real animal protein without the animal.

(2) The process begins by harvesting stem cells from a living animal via a harmless biopsy. These cells are placed in large steel vessels called "bioreactors," which mimic the conditions inside an animal's body. Fed a nutrient-rich soup of amino acids, sugars, and vitamins, the cells multiply and differentiate into muscle and fat tissue. The result is biologically identical to beef, chicken, or pork, but created with a fraction of the environmental footprint and zero antibiotics.

(3) Despite the scientific breakthrough, hurdles remain. The cost of production, though falling, is still higher than conventional meat. Furthermore, the industry faces a significant cultural barrier: the "ick factor." Many consumers view lab-grown food as unnatural or "Franken-meat." Proponents argue that eating a burger produced in a sterile lab is far cleaner and more ethical than one from a crowded, industrial slaughterhouse. The success of this revolution depends not just on science, but on the appetite of the public to embrace a new definition of meat.

questions.question: Based on Text 2, the author's main concern regarding "deepfakes" is ....

options.label: A
options.text: the technology is not advanced enough to be convincing

options.label: B
options.text: the erosion of shared reality and the potential for the "liar's dividend" to undermine truth

options.label: C
options.text: the use of deepfakes in Hollywood movies to replace actors

options.label: D
options.text: the high cost of creating deepfake videos

options.label: E
options.text: the lack of high-quality cameras

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 2: The Liar's Dividend
contentBlock.content:
(1) "Seeing is believing" has been the cornerstone of human trust for millennia. However, the rise of "deepfakes"—synthetic media generated by Artificial Intelligence—is rapidly eroding this foundation. Today, anyone with a powerful computer can swap faces in a video or clone a voice with terrifying accuracy. While the potential for fraud and disinformation is obvious, a more subtle danger lurks: the "liar's dividend."

(2) The liar's dividend refers to the benefit that dishonest actors reap from the mere existence of deepfakes. As the public becomes aware that videos can be forged, it becomes easier for politicians or criminals to dismiss genuine incriminating evidence as "fake news" or "AI-generated." If everything *can* be fake, then nothing has to be true. This creates a nihilistic information ecosystem where objective reality is up for debate.

(3) The consequences are profound. Trust in institutions, journalism, and the legal system relies on shared facts. Deepfakes threaten to fragment society into skeptical tribes, each believing only the "evidence" that confirms their bias. Combating this requires more than just detection software; it demands a new form of critical literacy. We must learn to verify sources, question context, and resist the urge to share content that triggers an immediate emotional response. In the age of AI, skepticism is a survival skill.

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 2: The Liar's Dividend
contentBlock.content:
(1) "Seeing is believing" has been the cornerstone of human trust for millennia. However, the rise of "deepfakes"—synthetic media generated by Artificial Intelligence—is rapidly eroding this foundation. Today, anyone with a powerful computer can swap faces in a video or clone a voice with terrifying accuracy. While the potential for fraud and disinformation is obvious, a more subtle danger lurks: the "liar's dividend."

(2) The liar's dividend refers to the benefit that dishonest actors reap from the mere existence of deepfakes. As the public becomes aware that videos can be forged, it becomes easier for politicians or criminals to dismiss genuine incriminating evidence as "fake news" or "AI-generated." If everything *can* be fake, then nothing has to be true. This creates a nihilistic information ecosystem where objective reality is up for debate.

(3) The consequences are profound. Trust in institutions, journalism, and the legal system relies on shared facts. Deepfakes threaten to fragment society into skeptical tribes, each believing only the "evidence" that confirms their bias. Combating this requires more than just detection software; it demands a new form of critical literacy. We must learn to verify sources, question context, and resist the urge to share content that triggers an immediate emotional response. In the age of AI, skepticism is a survival skill.

options.label: A
options.text: a literal building on the coast

options.label: B
options.text: the protagonist's isolation and his role as a guardian of truth/connection in a chaotic world

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 3: The Keeper of the Analog Light
contentBlock.content:
(1) The lighthouse stood on the edge of the world, a finger of white stone pointing at the gray sky. Inside, Silas polished the brass gears of the Fresnel lens. It was a ritual he had performed for forty years, the smell of kerosene and salt deeply embedded in his skin.

(2) "Why do you bother, Silas?" his nephew had asked via a video call, his face pixelated on the tablet screen. "Ships have GPS. They have satellite navigation. They don't need a light in a tower anymore."

(3) Silas looked out the window at the churning ocean. He knew the boy was right about the ships. But he wasn't keeping the light for the cargo tankers. He was keeping it for something else. In a world drowning in digital noise—a fog of notifications, pings, and data streams—he felt that humanity was losing its ability to navigate. People drifted, eyes glued to screens, crashing into invisible reefs of anxiety and loneliness.

(4) "The light is a constant," Silas murmured to the empty room. "It doesn't blink for attention. It doesn't ask for a like. It just is."

(5) As night fell, he climbed the spiral stairs. He lit the mantle. The beam swept across the dark water, a rhythmic, silent pulse. *Flash. Darkness. Flash. Darkness.* It was a heartbeat. Silas imagined the lost souls out there in the digital sea, looking up from their devices for just a second, seeing the beam, and remembering that there was still a solid shore, a place where things were real, tangible, and true.

questions.question: Based on Text 4, the reviewer’s overall opinion of the "Lingua-X" app is ....

options.label: A
options.text: it is a revolutionary tool that replaces human teachers perfectly

options.label: B
options.text: it is an engaging game that builds vocabulary but fails to teach true fluency or grammar depth

options.label: C
options.text: it is a boring app that no one should use

options.label: D
options.text: it is the most expensive app on the market

options.label: E
options.text: it is dangerous for children's brains

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 4: Review: Lingua-X App
contentBlock.content:
(1) The mascot of "Lingua-X"—a cheerful green parrot—is arguably the most persistent teacher on the planet. The app has gamified language learning to an art form. With its colorful interface, satisfying "ding" sounds, and the dreaded "streak" counter, it is incredibly effective at getting you to open the app every day. As a tool for building a habit, it is unrivaled.

(2) However, does it actually teach you a language? The answer is complicated. Lingua-X excels at vocabulary building and basic sentence structure. It lowers the barrier to entry, making a daunting task feel accessible. Yet, its reliance on translation exercises and bizarre, decontextualized sentences ("The horse eats the apple") creates an illusion of competence. Users can rack up thousands of points without being able to hold a basic conversation in the real world.

(3) The app focuses on passive recognition rather than active production. It lacks the messiness of real speech—the slang, the speed, the cultural nuance. It teaches you to pass a quiz, not to communicate with a human. Lingua-X is a fantastic supplement, a digital flashcard deck for the modern age. But relying on it as your sole method of learning is like thinking you can learn to play football just by playing FIFA. It’s fun, but don't expect to win the World Cup.

(2) However, does it actually teach you a language? The answer is complicated. Lingua-X excels at vocabulary building and basic sentence structure. It lowers the barrier to entry, making a daunting task feel accessible. Yet, its reliance on translation exercises and bizarre, decontextualized sentences ("The horse eats the apple") creates an illusion of competence. Users can rack up thousands of points without being able to hold a basic conversation in the real world.

(3) The app focuses on passive recognition rather than active production. It lacks the messiness of real speech—the slang, the speed, the cultural nuance. It teaches you to pass a quiz, not to communicate with a human. Lingua-X is a fantastic supplement, a digital flashcard deck for the modern age. But relying on it as your sole method of learning is like thinking you can learn to play football just by playing FIFA. It’s fun, but don't expect to win the World Cup.

questions.question: Based on Text 1, the author's primary purpose in the passage is to ....

options.label: A
options.text: provide a recipe for cooking lab-grown burgers

options.label: B
options.text: explain the scientific process, environmental benefits, and social hurdles of cultured meat

options.label: C
options.text: argue that vegetarianism is the only solution to climate change

options.label: D
options.text: criticize the taste of traditional beef compared to plant-based meat

options.label: E
options.text: describe the history of cattle farming in the 20th century

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 1: The Cultured Meat Revolution
contentBlock.content:
(1) For centuries, eating meat meant raising, feeding, and slaughtering a whole animal. This process is resource-intensive: livestock farming occupies nearly 80% of global agricultural land and is a major source of methane, a potent greenhouse gas. Enter "cultured meat," also known as lab-grown or cultivated meat. This technology promises to produce real animal protein without the animal.

(2) The process begins by harvesting stem cells from a living animal via a harmless biopsy. These cells are placed in large steel vessels called "bioreactors," which mimic the conditions inside an animal's body. Fed a nutrient-rich soup of amino acids, sugars, and vitamins, the cells multiply and differentiate into muscle and fat tissue. The result is biologically identical to beef, chicken, or pork, but created with a fraction of the environmental footprint and zero antibiotics.

(3) Despite the scientific breakthrough, hurdles remain. The cost of production, though falling, is still higher than conventional meat. Furthermore, the industry faces a significant cultural barrier: the "ick factor." Many consumers view lab-grown food as unnatural or "Franken-meat." Proponents argue that eating a burger produced in a sterile lab is far cleaner and more ethical than one from a crowded, industrial slaughterhouse. The success of this revolution depends not just on science, but on the appetite of the public to embrace a new definition of meat.

options.label: C

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 1: Mining with Plants
contentBlock.content:
(1) As high-grade metal ores become scarcer and mining digs deeper into the Earth, the environmental toll of extraction rises. A greener alternative is emerging from an unlikely source: plants. "Phytomining" is a technology that utilizes "hyperaccumulator" plants—species that have evolved to thrive in metal-rich soils by sucking up toxic metals like nickel, cobalt, and zinc through their roots. Instead of digging vast open-pit mines, we could simply farm these metals.

(2) The process is elegant in its simplicity. These specialized plants are grown on soil that contains low concentrations of metal (sub-economic for traditional mining) or on mine tailings (waste). As they grow, they bio-accumulate the metal ions in their cellular structure, primarily in the shoots and leaves. Once fully grown, the plants are harvested and burned. The energy generated from burning can be used for electricity, while the remaining ash serves as a high-grade "bio-ore," containing up to 20% pure metal, which can be smelted with minimal energy.

(3) While phytomining cannot match the sheer volume of output of industrial mining, it offers a dual benefit: remediation and extraction. It cleans up polluted sites by removing toxins from the ground while simultaneously producing value. However, the technology is limited by the growth rate of plants and the depth of roots, meaning it is a slow, seasonal harvest rather than a rapid excavation.

questions.question: Based on Text 2, the author's tone regarding the "Right to Repair" movement is ....

options.label: A
options.text: skeptical and dismissive

options.label: B
options.text: advocating and urgent

options.label: C
options.text: neutral and indifferent

options.label: D
options.text: fearful and cautionary

options.label: E
options.text: sarcastic and mocking

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 2: The Right to Repair
contentBlock.content:
(1) You bought your smartphone. You paid for it. But do you truly own it? The modern tech industry suggests the answer is "no." Manufacturers have increasingly designed products with "planned obsolescence" in mind, using proprietary screws, gluing batteries to casings, and software-locking components to prevent third-party repair. This creates a monopoly where the only option for a broken device is an expensive authorized repair or, more likely, buying a new one.

(2) This cycle is not just anti-consumer; it is an environmental disaster. Electronic waste (e-waste) is the fastest-growing waste stream in the world. By making devices difficult to fix, companies are forcing perfectly functional electronics into landfills. The "Right to Repair" movement argues that legislation is needed to force manufacturers to release repair manuals, spare parts, and diagnostic tools to the public.

(3) Tech giants argue that restricting repair is necessary for "safety" and "security," claiming that unauthorized batteries might explode or hackers might compromise hardware. While safety is valid, it is often used as a smokescreen to protect profit margins. Cars are far more dangerous than phones, yet independent mechanics are allowed to fix them. If we want a sustainable future, we must reclaim the right to tinker, fix, and extend the life of the things we own.

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 2: The Right to Repair
contentBlock.content:
(1) You bought your smartphone. You paid for it. But do you truly own it? The modern tech industry suggests the answer is "no." Manufacturers have increasingly designed products with "planned obsolescence" in mind, using proprietary screws, gluing batteries to casings, and software-locking components to prevent third-party repair. This creates a monopoly where the only option for a broken device is an expensive authorized repair or, more likely, buying a new one.

(2) This cycle is not just anti-consumer; it is an environmental disaster. Electronic waste (e-waste) is the fastest-growing waste stream in the world. By making devices difficult to fix, companies are forcing perfectly functional electronics into landfills. The "Right to Repair" movement argues that legislation is needed to force manufacturers to release repair manuals, spare parts, and diagnostic tools to the public.

(3) Tech giants argue that restricting repair is necessary for "safety" and "security," claiming that unauthorized batteries might explode or hackers might compromise hardware. While safety is valid, it is often used as a smokescreen to protect profit margins. Cars are far more dangerous than phones, yet independent mechanics are allowed to fix them. If we want a sustainable future, we must reclaim the right to tinker, fix, and extend the life of the things we own.

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 3: The Sound Collector
contentBlock.content:
(1) The sign on the door was small and etched in brass: "Elias Vane – Aural Antiquities." Inside, the shop was lined not with books or clocks, but with rows of sealed glass jars. To a casual observer, they looked empty. But Elias knew better. He walked the aisles, his fingers brushing the cool glass.

(2) A customer entered, a woman draped in expensive silk. "I am looking for something... soothing," she said, her voice tight with stress. "The city is so loud. I cannot sleep."

(3) Elias nodded. He bypassed the jars labeled "Rain on a Tin Roof" and "Distant Thunder." He reached for a small, dusty vial on the top shelf. "This," he whispered, "is the sound of the First Snowfall in a Pine Forest, recorded in 1952. Before the highways were built."

(4) He placed the jar on the counter. The woman looked skeptical. "It looks empty."

(5) "Open it only when you are in bed," Elias instructed. "It is not a sound you hear with your ears, but with your bones. It is the sound of the world holding its breath."

(6) Later, a collector came in, offering a fortune for "The Last Gasp of a Dying Star." Elias refused. He didn't collect sounds to hoard them for status; he collected them to save them from the noise. In a world that screamed for attention, he was the guardian of the whisper, the keeper of the quiet moments that humanity was too busy to notice before they vanished forever.

questions.question: Based on Text 4, the reviewer’s overall assessment of the "Hyper-loop X" train system is ....

options.label: A
options.text: it is a complete failure that should be abandoned

options.label: B
options.text: it is a theoretically brilliant transportation solution that is currently hampered by practical discomfort and cost

options.label: C
options.text: it is the most comfortable way to travel

options.label: D
options.text: it is slower than a conventional train

options.label: E
options.text: it is perfect in every way

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 4: Review: Hyper-loop X
contentBlock.content:
(1) The concept of the hyperloop—levitating pods traveling through vacuum tubes at airline speeds—has long been the holy grail of transport. The newly unveiled "Hyper-loop X" connecting Los Angeles to San Francisco claims to have cracked the code. After a test ride, I can confirm that the engineering is spectacular, but the human experience leaves much to be desired.

(2) First, the speed is undeniable. We covered 350 miles in 30 minutes. The magnetic levitation ensures a silent, friction-less glide that feels like magic. The carbon footprint is zero, powered entirely by solar panels lining the tube. It is a triumph of green engineering.

(3) However, the ride itself is an exercise in claustrophobic efficiency. To maintain structural integrity against the vacuum, the pods are windowless cylinders. Screens mimic the outside world, but the brain isn't fooled. The acceleration force (G-force) is intense, pressing you into your seat like a rocket launch, which led to visible nausea among several passengers. Furthermore, the ticket price is currently rivaling a first-class flight, making it a toy for the elite rather than public transit. The Hyper-loop X is undeniably the future of cargo, but until it solves the comfort and cost issues, humans might prefer the slower, scenic route of a traditional train.

contentBlocks.content:

options.label: A
options.text: explain the chemical composition of nickel and cobalt found in soil

options.label: B
options.text: describe a sustainable method of mining that utilizes plants to extract metals from the earth

options.label: C
options.text: argue against the use of traditional mining because it is too expensive

options.label: D
options.text: promote the cultivation of hyperaccumulator plants for decorative gardening

options.label: E
options.text: list the different species of plants that can survive in toxic environments

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 1: Mining with Plants
contentBlock.content:
(1) As high-grade metal ores become scarcer and mining digs deeper into the Earth, the environmental toll of extraction rises. A greener alternative is emerging from an unlikely source: plants. "Phytomining" is a technology that utilizes "hyperaccumulator" plants—species that have evolved to thrive in metal-rich soils by sucking up toxic metals like nickel, cobalt, and zinc through their roots. Instead of digging vast open-pit mines, we could simply farm these metals.

(2) The process is elegant in its simplicity. These specialized plants are grown on soil that contains low concentrations of metal (sub-economic for traditional mining) or on mine tailings (waste). As they grow, they bio-accumulate the metal ions in their cellular structure, primarily in the shoots and leaves. Once fully grown, the plants are harvested and burned. The energy generated from burning can be used for electricity, while the remaining ash serves as a high-grade "bio-ore," containing up to 20% pure metal, which can be smelted with minimal energy.

(3) While phytomining cannot match the sheer volume of output of industrial mining, it offers a dual benefit: remediation and extraction. It cleans up polluted sites by removing toxins from the ground while simultaneously producing value. However, the technology is limited by the growth rate of plants and the depth of roots, meaning it is a slow, seasonal harvest rather than a rapid excavation.

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 1: The Rise of Bio-bots
contentBlock.content:
(1) The field of robotics is undergoing a soft revolution. For decades, robots were defined by rigid metal skeletons, hydraulic pistons, and electric motors—movements that were precise but stiff. Now, scientists are pioneering "bio-hybrid" robots, machines that integrate living biological tissue with synthetic materials. The goal is to create devices that move with the fluid grace of animals and possess the unique self-healing properties of living organisms.

(2) One of the most promising designs involves using lab-grown skeletal muscle cells as "actuators" (the movers) attached to a 3D-printed flexible skeleton. When stimulated by an electric pulse or light, the muscle tissue contracts, pulling the skeleton and generating movement. Unlike mechanical motors, which wear out and break, living muscle tissue is energy-efficient and can repair minor tears on its own, provided it is supplied with nutrients.

(3) These bio-bots have successfully walked, swum, and even gripped objects in laboratory settings. The potential applications are vast, from microsurgery bots that can navigate the human body without damaging tissue to environmental sensors that biodegradable after their mission. However, a major hurdle remains: keeping the biological components alive. Currently, these robots mostly operate in nutrient-rich fluids, limiting their use in dry, real-world environments.

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 2: The Perils of Sharenting
contentBlock.content:
(1) The first ultrasound. The first step. The first day of school. For modern parents, sharing these milestones on social media is almost a reflex. This phenomenon, dubbed "sharenting," seems like a harmless way to update family and friends. However, privacy experts warn that we are creating a permanent "digital shadow" for our children before they can even speak, let alone consent.

(2) By the time an average child turns five, parents have posted over 1,000 photos of them online. This data accumulation is not benign. It exposes children to risks ranging from digital kidnapping (strangers using photos for roleplay) to future identity theft, as birthdates and pet names are often revealed in captions. More philosophically, it robs the child of the right to autonomy. They inherit a digital identity constructed by their parents, complete with embarrassing moments that may haunt them in professional or social settings as adults.

(3) Parents argue that they own the right to share their own lives, which includes their children. Yet, this perspective treats the child as property rather than an individual with distinct privacy rights. The dopamine hit of "likes" and comments should not outweigh the duty to protect a vulnerable subject. We need a cultural shift where the child's privacy takes precedence over parental validation.

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 2: The Perils of Sharenting
contentBlock.content:
(1) The first ultrasound. The first step. The first day of school. For modern parents, sharing these milestones on social media is almost a reflex. This phenomenon, dubbed "sharenting," seems like a harmless way to update family and friends. However, privacy experts warn that we are creating a permanent "digital shadow" for our children before they can even speak, let alone consent.

(2) By the time an average child turns five, parents have posted over 1,000 photos of them online. This data accumulation is not benign. It exposes children to risks ranging from digital kidnapping (strangers using photos for roleplay) to future identity theft, as birthdates and pet names are often revealed in captions. More philosophically, it robs the child of the right to autonomy. They inherit a digital identity constructed by their parents, complete with embarrassing moments that may haunt them in professional or social settings as adults.

(3) Parents argue that they own the right to share their own lives, which includes their children. Yet, this perspective treats the child as property rather than an individual with distinct privacy rights. The dopamine hit of "likes" and comments should not outweigh the duty to protect a vulnerable subject. We need a cultural shift where the child's privacy takes precedence over parental validation.

options.label: A
options.text: the decay of the garden

options.label: B
options.text: the protagonist's fading memory and loss of her defining sense (smell)

options.label: C
options.text: the changing of the seasons from summer to autumn

options.label: D
options.text: the poor quality of the perfume

options.label: E

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 3: The Scent Keeper
contentBlock.content:
(1) Elara stood in her laboratory, surrounded by amber vials of essential oils—bergamot, vetiver, oud, and rose. For twenty years, she had been the city's most celebrated "Nose," creating perfumes that could evoke a forgotten summer or the sorrow of a lost love. But today, as she uncorked a bottle of potent jasmine absolute, she smelled... nothing.

(2) The silence of her senses had started a month ago, a gradual fading like a radio losing signal. First the subtle top notes of citrus vanished, then the floral heart. Now, even the heavy base notes were gone. Panic, cold and sharp, clawed at her throat. A musician could still hear the music in their head; a painter could see the colors. But scent? Scent was visceral, immediate. Without it, she was a ghost in her own life.

(3) She had one final commission to finish: "The Scent of Memory" for a dying billionaire who wanted to smell his childhood home one last time. Elara closed her eyes. She couldn't smell the ingredients, but she remembered them. She remembered the metallic tang of rain on hot asphalt, the warmth of worn leather, the sweetness of dried figs. Trembling, she began to mix the oils, measuring by weight, trusting the formula written in her mind. It was like painting in the dark. She bottled the mixture, praying that her memory was truer than her failing senses.

questions.question: Based on Text 4, the reviewer’s overall assessment of the "Iris-X" smart contact lens is ....

options.label: A
options.text: it is a flawless device that everyone should buy

options.label: B
options.text: it is a technological breakthrough with impressive navigation features but significant privacy and power drawbacks

options.label: C
options.text: it is a dangerous medical device that blinds people

options.label: D
options.text: it is a cheap toy with no real utility

options.label: E
options.text: it is the best camera ever made

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 4: Review: The Iris-X Smart Lens
contentBlock.content:
(1) Science fiction has long promised us the ability to layer digital information over the physical world, and the "Iris-X" smart contact lens finally attempts to deliver on that promise. After a week of wearing this prototype, I am simultaneously amazed and unsettled. The technology is essentially invisible: a micro-display embedded in a soft hydrogel lens that sits comfortably on your eye.

(2) The utility is undeniable. Walking through Tokyo, the Iris-X translated street signs in real-time, overlaying English text directly onto the metal signposts. Navigation is seamless; instead of looking down at a phone map, floating green arrows appear on the sidewalk to guide you. It removes the "screen barrier" that has defined the smartphone era, allowing you to stay present in the world while accessing data.

(3) However, the Iris-X is not without significant flaws. The battery life is abysmal, barely lasting four hours of active use. More concerning are the privacy implications. The lens includes a microscopic camera for image recognition. While there is a tiny LED that glows when recording, it is easily missed. Wearing it feels like being a walking surveillance camera, and I noticed people becoming visibly uncomfortable when I maintained eye contact. The Iris-X is a technological marvel, but until the battery improves and the social etiquette of "face-worn cameras" is resolved, it remains a niche gadget for early adopters rather than a consumer revolution.

options.text: warn against the dangers of creating Frankenstein-like monsters

options.label: B
options.text: describe the manufacturing process of traditional metal robots

options.label: C
options.text: explain the emergence of bio-hybrid robotics as a field that merges synthetic and biological materials to overcome the limitations of rigid machines

options.label: D

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 1: The Rise of Bio-bots
contentBlock.content:
(1) The field of robotics is undergoing a soft revolution. For decades, robots were defined by rigid metal skeletons, hydraulic pistons, and electric motors—movements that were precise but stiff. Now, scientists are pioneering "bio-hybrid" robots, machines that integrate living biological tissue with synthetic materials. The goal is to create devices that move with the fluid grace of animals and possess the unique self-healing properties of living organisms.

(2) One of the most promising designs involves using lab-grown skeletal muscle cells as "actuators" (the movers) attached to a 3D-printed flexible skeleton. When stimulated by an electric pulse or light, the muscle tissue contracts, pulling the skeleton and generating movement. Unlike mechanical motors, which wear out and break, living muscle tissue is energy-efficient and can repair minor tears on its own, provided it is supplied with nutrients.

(3) These bio-bots have successfully walked, swum, and even gripped objects in laboratory settings. The potential applications are vast, from microsurgery bots that can navigate the human body without damaging tissue to environmental sensors that biodegradable after their mission. However, a major hurdle remains: keeping the biological components alive. Currently, these robots mostly operate in nutrient-rich fluids, limiting their use in dry, real-world environments.

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 1: The Wood Wide Web
contentBlock.content:
(1) Beneath the forest floor, a hidden drama unfolds that challenges our understanding of nature as a brutal competition for survival. Scientists have mapped a vast, underground network of fungi known as mycorrhizae. These fungi attach themselves to tree roots, creating a symbiotic superhighway often dubbed the "Wood Wide Web." Through this network, trees do not merely coexist; they communicate, trade, and even wage war.

(2) The relationship is a classic mutualism: the fungi provide the trees with hard-to-access soil nutrients like phosphorus and nitrogen, which they extract using their enzymes. In return, the trees donate a significant portion of their carbon-based sugars, produced via photosynthesis, to the fungi. But the exchange goes deeper. Research indicates that trees use this fungal internet to share resources with their neighbors.

(3) Perhaps most poignant is the discovery of "Mother Trees"—the largest, oldest trees in the forest. These matriarchs act as hubs, connected to hundreds of other trees. Isotopes show that Mother Trees pump sugar into shaded seedlings to help them survive, and can even send chemical distress signals when under attack by beetles, prompting neighbors to raise their defensive barriers. This suggests that a forest is not a collection of isolated individuals, but a single, integrated super-organism.

options.label: B
options.text: it has inadvertently harmed low-income communities by raising prices and reducing the quality of available goods

options.label: C

options.text: it creates high-paying jobs for everyone involved

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 2: The Gentrification of Thrift
contentBlock.content:
(1) Thrifting was once a survival strategy for the working class—a way to clothe a family when retail prices were out of reach. Today, it is a viral aesthetic. Scroll through TikTok or Instagram, and you will see influencers hauling bags of "vintage" finds. While the destigmatization of second-hand shopping is a win for sustainability, this boom has birthed a controversial economic phenomenon: the gentrification of thrift stores.

(2) The rise of resale platforms like Depop has created a new class of professional resellers. These individuals treat local charity shops as wholesalers, buying up high-quality stock in bulk to resell online at a 300% markup. This aggressive arbitrage alters the ecosystem. Charity shops, noticing the demand, have begun raising their prices to match "market value." A t-shirt that once cost $2 now costs $15, effectively pricing out the low-income community that relies on these stores.

(3) Defenders argue that resellers are saving clothes from landfills. However, this ignores the displacement effect. When the "good stuff" is stripped away by professionals, local shoppers are left with the fast-fashion junk that falls apart after one wash. Thrifting has morphed from a necessity into a performative hobby for the affluent, leaving the poor to shoulder the burden of a market that no longer serves them.
