While the destigmatization of second-hand shopping is a win for sustainability, this boom has birthed a controversial economic phenomenon: the gentrification of thrift stores.

(2) The rise of resale platforms like Depop has created a new class of professional resellers. These individuals treat local charity shops as wholesalers, buying up high-quality stock in bulk to resell online at a 300% markup. This aggressive arbitrage alters the ecosystem. Charity shops, noticing the demand, have begun raising their prices to match "market value." A t-shirt that once cost $2 now costs $15, effectively pricing out the low-income community that relies on these stores.

(3) Defenders argue that resellers are saving clothes from landfills. However, this ignores the displacement effect. When the "good stuff" is stripped away by professionals, local shoppers are left with the fast-fashion junk that falls apart after one wash. Thrifting has morphed from a necessity into a performative hobby for the affluent, leaving the poor to shoulder the burden of a market that no longer serves them.

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 3: The Last Glaciologist
contentBlock.content:
(1) Dr. Aris kneeled on the jagged rock, checking the depth marker he had drilled into the ice six months ago. It stood a full meter above the surface now, a tombstone marking how much the Okjökull glacier had retreated. Around him, the ice groaned, a deep, resonant cracking sound that echoed through the valley. To Aris, it didn't sound like geology; it sounded like a dying breath.

(2) "She’s bleeding faster this year," Aris muttered, recording the data into his tablet. He used to think of himself as a scientist, an objective observer of the cryosphere. Lately, he felt more like a hospice nurse, documenting the final days of a terminal patient.

(3) The meltwater ran in rivets around his boots, carrying with it ancient dust and trapped air bubbles from ten thousand years ago. The smell was distinctive—metallic and cold. Near the edge of the retreat, something dark protruded from the slush. Aris walked over. It was a leather boot, perfectly preserved, its laces still tied. A relic from a climber lost decades ago, now exhaled by the shrinking ice.

(4) Aris looked up at the peak, now gray and exposed where it used to be white. He realized that the tragedy wasn't just the loss of water or the rise of sea levels. It was the erasure of memory. As the ice vanished, it took with it the climatic history of the world, melting the library of the earth before anyone had a chance to read the final chapter.

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 4: Review: EduVerse VR
contentBlock.content:
(1) The promise of Virtual Reality (VR) in education is seductive: why read about the Colosseum when you can stand in the center of the arena? The "EduVerse" headset attempts to fulfill this promise, marketing itself as the future of the classroom. After a month-long pilot program in a high school history class, the verdict is a mixed bag of awe-inspiring potential and frustrating limitations.

(2) The strengths are undeniable. When the system works, the immersion is total. Students who were previously bored by textbook diagrams lit up when they could manipulate 3D DNA strands in mid-air. The ability to visualize abstract concepts is EduVerse’s killer app. It turns passive learning into active exploration.

(3) However, the hardware is not ready for the bell schedule. The headset is heavy, causing neck strain after just 20 minutes. Motion sickness remains a significant hurdle; three students had to leave the room due to nausea during a "flight" over Ancient Egypt. Furthermore, the isolation is problematic. Education is inherently social, relying on the subtle cues between teacher and student. Inside the headset, students are cut off from the room, turning a collective experience into a solitary one. The novelty wears off quickly, leaving behind a heavy piece of plastic that is more of a distraction than a tool. Until the tech becomes invisible, it will remain a gimmick rather than a revolution.

options.label: A
options.text: the internet connection available in remote forest areas

options.label: B
options.text: the visible network of roots that trip hikers on forest trails

options.label: C
options.text: the underground fungal network that facilitates communication and resource exchange between trees

options.label: D
options.text: the spider webs that connect trees in dense canopies

options.label: E
options.text: the digital mapping of forest density using satellite imagery

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 1: The Wood Wide Web
contentBlock.content:
(1) Beneath the forest floor, a hidden drama unfolds that challenges our understanding of nature as a brutal competition for survival. Scientists have mapped a vast, underground network of fungi known as mycorrhizae. These fungi attach themselves to tree roots, creating a symbiotic superhighway often dubbed the "Wood Wide Web." Through this network, trees do not merely coexist; they communicate, trade, and even wage war.

(2) The relationship is a classic mutualism: the fungi provide the trees with hard-to-access soil nutrients like phosphorus and nitrogen, which they extract using their enzymes. In return, the trees donate a significant portion of their carbon-based sugars, produced via photosynthesis, to the fungi. But the exchange goes deeper. Research indicates that trees use this fungal internet to share resources with their neighbors.

(3) Perhaps most poignant is the discovery of "Mother Trees"—the largest, oldest trees in the forest. These matriarchs act as hubs, connected to hundreds of other trees. Isotopes show that Mother Trees pump sugar into shaded seedlings to help them survive, and can even send chemical distress signals when under attack by beetles, prompting neighbors to raise their defensive barriers. This suggests that a forest is not a collection of isolated individuals, but a single, integrated super-organism.

options.label: A
options.text: Large electric fans force cold air into the building from the roof.

options.label: B
options.text: Cool air is drawn in from the bottom of the building through vents.

options.label: C

options.label: D

options.label: E

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 1: The Termite Architect
contentBlock.content:
(1) In the blistering heat of Harare, Zimbabwe, stands a building that defies conventional engineering logic. The Eastgate Centre, a mid-rise shopping and office complex, uses less than 10% of the energy consumed by similar conventional buildings. Its secret lies not in high-tech solar panels or advanced air conditioning, but in the humble termite mound. The architect, Mick Pearce, utilized the principles of biomimicry—innovation inspired by nature—to solve the problem of cooling a massive structure without fossil fuels.

(2) Termites in Africa build towering mounds that maintain a constant internal temperature of 31°C, essential for the fungus they farm, even while outside temperatures swing from 3°C to 42°C. They achieve this through an ingenious system of passive cooling. The mound acts as a giant lung. Hot air rises and escapes through a central chimney, drawing cooler air in from the bottom through a network of tunnels.

(3) Pearce replicated this by designing the Eastgate Centre with a high "thermal mass." The building is made of concrete and brick, materials that absorb heat during the day and release it at night. Fans suck cool night air into the bottom of the building, chilling the concrete structure. During the day, as the outside temperature rises, the cool concrete absorbs the heat from the offices. The warm air then rises through vertical vents and is expelled through chimneys on the roof. This reliance on diurnal temperature fluctuation proves that sometimes, the most advanced technology is biology itself.

Hot air rises and escapes through a central chimney, drawing cooler air in from the bottom through a network of tunnels.

(3) Pearce replicated this by designing the Eastgate Centre with a high "thermal mass." The building is made of concrete and brick, materials that absorb heat during the day and release it at night. Fans suck cool night air into the bottom of the building, chilling the concrete structure. During the day, as the outside temperature rises, the cool concrete absorbs the heat from the offices. The warm air then rises through vertical vents and is expelled through chimneys on the roof. This reliance on diurnal temperature fluctuation proves that sometimes, the most advanced technology is biology itself.

options.label: B

options.label: C

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 2: The Validation Trap
contentBlock.content:
(1) The architecture of modern social media is not accidental; it is a carefully calibrated psychological engine designed to keep users engaged. At the heart of this engine is the "validation loop." Every time we post a photo and receive a "like," our brain releases a hit of dopamine, the same neurotransmitter associated with gambling and substance use. This creates a powerful reinforcement mechanism: we post, we get rewarded, we post again.

(2) However, this system has a dark underbelly. It fosters a culture of relentless social comparison. When we scroll through our feeds, we are not seeing reality; we are seeing a "highlight reel"—a curated, filtered, and often staged version of other people's lives. We see the vacations, the promotions, and the perfect relationships, but never the mundane struggles or failures.

(3) This "curated reality" creates a distorted baseline for normalcy. Users, particularly adolescents, begin to feel that their own lives are inadequate by comparison. The self-worth of an individual becomes tethered to external metrics of engagement. When the likes stop coming, the withdrawal brings anxiety and a sense of invisibility. To reclaim our mental health, we must break the loop. This involves "digital mindfulness"—recognizing that the screen is a stage, not a mirror, and that our value exists independently of an algorithm's approval.

options.label: A
options.text: a recycling center for old newspapers

options.label: B
options.text: a sanctuary for preserving human history and tangible knowledge in a digitized, ephemeral world

options.label: C
options.text: a governmental office for printing laws

options.label: D
options.text: a place where paper is manufactured

options.label: E
options.text: a gallery displaying origami art

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 3: The Paper Museum
contentBlock.content:
(1) The sign above the door was barely legible: "The Paper Museum." Inside, the air was thick with the scent of vanilla, almond, and decay—the smell of old lignin breaking down. Elara took a deep breath, letting the aroma fill her lungs. It was a stark contrast to the sterile, ozone-scented air of the city outside, where everything was glass, chrome, and holograms.

(2) "Please, don't touch the exhibits," the curator, an old man named Hideo, said gently. He was dusting a glass case containing a 21st-century hardcover novel.

(3) Elara approached the case. "They used to carry these?" she asked, marveling at the thickness of the object. "It looks... heavy."

(4) "Knowledge used to have weight," Hideo replied. "It had texture. You could feel how far you had journeyed in a story by the weight of the pages in your left hand versus your right. Now, a library fits on a chip the size of a fingernail. We have gained efficiency, yes, but we have lost the geography of reading."

(5) Elara looked at her own translucent wrist-pad, blinking with notifications. The digital world was fast, fluid, and forgettable. Here, in the dim light, the silence felt heavy and significant. She watched Hideo turn a page. The sound—a dry, crisp crackle—was louder than the humming of the hover-cars outside. A young boy walked in, eyes wide, staring at a wall of maps. Elara smiled. As long as this room existed, the anchor to the past would hold, keeping them from drifting away into the ether of pure data.

options.text: caused the government to shut down the museum

options.label: E
options.text: proved that young people cannot focus on reading

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 3: The Paper Museum
contentBlock.content:
(1) The sign above the door was barely legible: "The Paper Museum." Inside, the air was thick with the scent of vanilla, almond, and decay—the smell of old lignin breaking down. Elara took a deep breath, letting the aroma fill her lungs. It was a stark contrast to the sterile, ozone-scented air of the city outside, where everything was glass, chrome, and holograms.

(2) "Please, don't touch the exhibits," the curator, an old man named Hideo, said gently. He was dusting a glass case containing a 21st-century hardcover novel.

(3) Elara approached the case. "They used to carry these?" she asked, marveling at the thickness of the object. "It looks... heavy."

(4) "Knowledge used to have weight," Hideo replied. "It had texture. You could feel how far you had journeyed in a story by the weight of the pages in your left hand versus your right. Now, a library fits on a chip the size of a fingernail. We have gained efficiency, yes, but we have lost the geography of reading."

(5) Elara looked at her own translucent wrist-pad, blinking with notifications. The digital world was fast, fluid, and forgettable. Here, in the dim light, the silence felt heavy and significant. She watched Hideo turn a page. The sound—a dry, crisp crackle—was louder than the humming of the hover-cars outside. A young boy walked in, eyes wide, staring at a wall of maps. Elara smiled. As long as this room existed, the anchor to the past would hold, keeping them from drifting away into the ether of pure data.

options.label: A
options.text: a utopian paradise that solves all urban problems

options.label: B
options.text: a technological marvel that is functionally impressive but socially dystopian and soulless

options.label: C
options.text: a complete financial scam that will never be built

options.label: D
options.text: a boring project that offers nothing new

options.label: E
options.text: the most eco-friendly city in existence

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 4: Review: NeuraLink City
contentBlock.content:
(1) The grand opening of NeuraLink City was marketed as the dawn of a new civilization. A fully autonomous, AI-driven metropolis built from scratch in the Nevada desert. After spending a week in its pristine streets, I can confirm that it is indeed a marvel of engineering. The "Zero-Waste" system is miraculous; trash disappears instantly into pneumatic tubes and is recycled with 100% efficiency. Traffic is non-existent, thanks to the swarm of self-driving pods that communicate seamlessly to optimize flow. The renewable energy grid is a triumph of sustainability.

(2) Yet, for all its functional perfection, NeuraLink City feels hauntingly empty. It is a city without a soul. The architectural uniformity—endless rows of white, biomorphic towers—creates a sense of disorientation. There are no alleyways, no graffiti, no "messy" corners where culture usually breeds. Every movement is watched by the omnipresent "Safety Eye" cameras, which supposedly ensure low crime but actually induce a feeling of constant performance.

(3) The efficiency is sanitized to the point of sterility. There are no street vendors because they disrupt the pedestrian flow algorithms. There is no noise, no friction, no spontaneity. It feels less like a community and more like a computer chip—optimized for processing, not for living. NeuraLink City proves that you can solve every logistical problem of urban life and still fail to create a place where people actually want to belong. It is a perfect machine, but a terrible home.

questions.question: Based on Text 1, the author's primary purpose in writing this passage is to ....

options.label: A
options.text: argue that termites are intelligent pests that destroy wooden structures

options.label: B
options.text: describe the structural similarities between termite mounds and human skyscrapers

options.label: C
options.text: explain how biomimicry utilizes nature's designs to solve modern architectural problems, specifically energy efficiency

options.label: D
options.text: promote a specific architect's biography and their contributions to African architecture

options.label: E
options.text: criticize modern air conditioning systems for being too expensive

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 1: The Termite Architect
contentBlock.content:
(1) In the blistering heat of Harare, Zimbabwe, stands a building that defies conventional engineering logic. The Eastgate Centre, a mid-rise shopping and office complex, uses less than 10% of the energy consumed by similar conventional buildings. Its secret lies not in high-tech solar panels or advanced air conditioning, but in the humble termite mound. The architect, Mick Pearce, utilized the principles of biomimicry—innovation inspired by nature—to solve the problem of cooling a massive structure without fossil fuels.

(2) Termites in Africa build towering mounds that maintain a constant internal temperature of 31°C, essential for the fungus they farm, even while outside temperatures swing from 3°C to 42°C. They achieve this through an ingenious system of passive cooling. The mound acts as a giant lung. Hot air rises and escapes through a central chimney, drawing cooler air in from the bottom through a network of tunnels.

(3) Pearce replicated this by designing the Eastgate Centre with a high "thermal mass." The building is made of concrete and brick, materials that absorb heat during the day and release it at night. Fans suck cool night air into the bottom of the building, chilling the concrete structure. During the day, as the outside temperature rises, the cool concrete absorbs the heat from the offices. The warm air then rises through vertical vents and is expelled through chimneys on the roof. This reliance on diurnal temperature fluctuation proves that sometimes, the most advanced technology is biology itself.

questions.question:
(Select the correct answers - there can be more than one)
Based on Text 1, which statements accurately describe the chemical process of bioluminescence?

options.label: A
options.text: It requires the absorption of significant heat from the environment to trigger the reaction.

options.label: B
options.text: It involves the oxidation of a molecule called luciferin.

options.label: C
options.text: The enzyme luciferase acts as a catalyst to speed up the reaction.

options.label: D
options.text: It produces light as a byproduct of generating intense heat.

options.label: E
options.text: It is often referred to as "cold light" because nearly all energy is converted into illumination.

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 1: Living Light
contentBlock.content:
(1) Bioluminescence is one of nature’s most dazzling phenomena, a process where living organisms produce and emit light. Unlike the heat-generating light of a campfire or an incandescent bulb, bioluminescence is a form of "cold light"—nearly 100% of the energy is converted into illumination with minimal heat loss. The chemistry behind this involves two key players: a light-emitting molecule called *luciferin* and an enzyme called *luciferase*. When luciferin reacts with oxygen, catalyzed by luciferase, it oxidizes to form oxyluciferin. This reaction releases energy in the form of photons, creating the glow we see in fireflies, fungi, and deep-sea creatures.

(2) In the pitch-black depths of the ocean, where sunlight cannot penetrate, bioluminescence serves critical survival functions. For the Anglerfish, a glowing lure dangling from its head acts as bait to attract unsuspecting prey. Conversely, some species use light for defense. The "burglar alarm" effect occurs when a threatened organism lights up to attract a larger predator that will attack the original aggressor. Others, like certain squids, utilize "counter-illumination," emitting light from their bellies to match the faint sunlight from above, effectively erasing their silhouette to predators lurking below.

(3) The versatility of this biological mechanism is a testament to convergent evolution, having evolved independently at least 40 times across different lineages. Today, scientists are harnessing this chemistry for medical research, using bioluminescent markers to track the spread of cancer cells or viral infections within living tissues, proving that this ancient survival tool can shed light on modern medical mysteries.

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 1: Living Light
contentBlock.content:
(1) Bioluminescence is one of nature’s most dazzling phenomena, a process where living organisms produce and emit light. Unlike the heat-generating light of a campfire or an incandescent bulb, bioluminescence is a form of "cold light"—nearly 100% of the energy is converted into illumination with minimal heat loss. The chemistry behind this involves two key players: a light-emitting molecule called *luciferin* and an enzyme called *luciferase*. When luciferin reacts with oxygen, catalyzed by luciferase, it oxidizes to form oxyluciferin. This reaction releases energy in the form of photons, creating the glow we see in fireflies, fungi, and deep-sea creatures.

(2) In the pitch-black depths of the ocean, where sunlight cannot penetrate, bioluminescence serves critical survival functions. For the Anglerfish, a glowing lure dangling from its head acts as bait to attract unsuspecting prey. Conversely, some species use light for defense. The "burglar alarm" effect occurs when a threatened organism lights up to attract a larger predator that will attack the original aggressor. Others, like certain squids, utilize "counter-illumination," emitting light from their bellies to match the faint sunlight from above, effectively erasing their silhouette to predators lurking below.

(3) The versatility of this biological mechanism is a testament to convergent evolution, having evolved independently at least 40 times across different lineages. Today, scientists are harnessing this chemistry for medical research, using bioluminescent markers to track the spread of cancer cells or viral infections within living tissues, proving that this ancient survival tool can shed light on modern medical mysteries.

questions.question: Based on Text 1, the relationship between "oxygen" and "light emission" in bioluminescence is best described as ....

contentBlock.title: Text 2: The Paradox of Choice
contentBlock.content:
(1) Modern society is built on the dogma that maximizing choice maximizes freedom and well-being. We assume that having 50 varieties of cereal or 100 shades of lipstick is a sign of a healthy, liberated consumer culture. However, psychologist Barry Schwartz argues that this abundance has tipped into a "tyranny of choice." Instead of liberating us, the explosion of options often leads to anxiety, paralysis, and dissatisfaction—a phenomenon known as the "Paradox of Choice."

(2) When faced with too many alternatives, we suffer from "analysis paralysis." The cognitive load required to evaluate every specification of every smartphone model is exhausting. Furthermore, the sheer volume of options raises our expectations. In a world of unlimited choice, we expect perfection. When the product we choose turns out to be merely "good" rather than "perfect," we blame ourselves for choosing poorly, leading to regret. This stands in stark contrast to an era of limited choice, where if a product was subpar, we blamed the manufacturer or the world, not ourselves.

(3) Schwartz distinguishes between "maximizers"—those who exhaustively seek the absolute best—and "satisficers"—those who settle for something that meets their standards. Maximizers, despite often making objectively better decisions, feel worse about them. The antidote to this modern malaise is not more options, but the cultivation of "satisficing." We must learn to embrace "good enough" and voluntarily limit our choices to reclaim our mental peace.

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 3: The Clockmaker's Silence
contentBlock.content:
(1) The shop smelled of oil, old mahogany, and patience. Silas Halloway sat hunched over his workbench, a jeweler’s loupe screwed into his eye socket. Around him, the walls were lined with hundreds of clocks—Grandfathers, cuckoos, mantle clocks, and pocket watches. They ticked in a chaotic, unsynchronized rhythm, a heartbeat of brass and steel that Silas found more comforting than the silence of the streets outside.

(2) "It’s obsolete, Silas," a man in a sharp suit had told him yesterday, pointing to the delicate gear train of a 19th-century chronometer. "People want smartwatches. They want notifications, heart rates, connectivity. Nobody cares about a spring that loses three seconds a month."

(3) Silas picked up a tiny hairspring with his tweezers. He didn't care about connectivity. He cared about the soul of the machine. A smartwatch was a screen that demanded attention; a mechanical watch was a companion that asked for nothing but movement.

(4) The bell above the door chimed. A young girl, no older than ten, walked in, holding a shattered pocket watch. "My grandpa gave me this," she whispered, her eyes wide. "He said it has a heartbeat. Can you fix it?"

(5) Silas took the watch. It was a ruin of bent gears and cracked glass. But he felt the weight of it, the history worn into the gold plating. He looked at the girl. He saw the same spark he had felt fifty years ago. "It will take time," Silas said, his voice raspy. "And patience. Do you have patience, child?" The girl nodded. Silas smiled, and for the first time that day, the ticking of the clocks didn't sound like a countdown, but like a beginning.

(5) Silas took the watch. It was a ruin of bent gears and cracked glass. But he felt the weight of it, the history worn into the gold plating. He looked at the girl. He saw the same spark he had felt fifty years ago. "It will take time," Silas said, his voice raspy. "And patience. Do you have patience, child?" The girl nodded. Silas smiled, and for the first time that day, the ticking of the clocks didn't sound like a countdown, but like a beginning.

contentBlock.title: Text 3: The Clockmaker's Silence
contentBlock.content:
(1) The shop smelled of oil, old mahogany, and patience. Silas Halloway sat hunched over his workbench, a jeweler’s loupe screwed into his eye socket. Around him, the walls were lined with hundreds of clocks—Grandfathers, cuckoos, mantle clocks, and pocket watches. They ticked in a chaotic, unsynchronized rhythm, a heartbeat of brass and steel that Silas found more comforting than the silence of the streets outside.

options.label: A
options.text: it is a dangerous device that should be banned immediately

options.label: B
options.text: it is a technological marvel that offers incredible convenience but at a terrifying privacy cost

options.label: C
options.text: it is a useless gadget that fails to perform basic functions

options.label: D
options.text: it is the most secure device on the market today

options.label: E
options.text: it is overpriced and not worth the money for the hardware alone

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 4: Review: The Omni-Link Assistant
contentBlock.content:
(1) The tech giants promised us a seamless future, and the new "Omni-Link" smart home hub delivers exactly that—along with a terrifying dose of Orwellian surveillance. On the surface, the device is a marvel. It unifies your lights, locks, thermostat, and entertainment systems into one intuitive interface. The voice recognition is uncanny; it understands context and nuance better than any human butler. The "Predictive Ordering" feature, which replenishes your groceries before you run out, feels like magic.

(2) However, the magic fades when you read the Terms of Service. The Omni-Link is not just a helper; it is a data vacuum. During my week of testing, I discovered that the device was recording audio snippets even when the "wake word" wasn't spoken, ostensibly to improve accuracy. More alamingly, this data is shared with "third-party partners" to build a consumer profile so detailed it knows my dietary habits better than my doctor.

(3) The convenience is undeniable, but the price is your privacy. By installing the Omni-Link, you are effectively building a digital Panopticon in your living room. It invites a corporate entity to watch, listen, and predict your behavior. For those who value privacy, this device is a Trojan horse. For those who value convenience above all else, it is the ultimate luxury. Just remember: when the product is this smart, *you* are the product being sold.

questions.question: Based on Text 1, the primary purpose of the passage is to ....

options.label: A
options.text: classify the different species of deep-sea creatures that emit light

options.label: B
options.text: explain the chemical mechanism of bioluminescence and its varied evolutionary functions

options.label: C
options.text: argue that bioluminescence is the most efficient form of energy production known to man

options.label: D
options.text: describe the mating rituals of fireflies in detail

options.label: E
options.text: warn about the dangers of swimming in bioluminescent bays

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 1: Living Light
contentBlock.content:
(1) Bioluminescence is one of nature’s most dazzling phenomena, a process where living organisms produce and emit light. Unlike the heat-generating light of a campfire or an incandescent bulb, bioluminescence is a form of "cold light"—nearly 100% of the energy is converted into illumination with minimal heat loss. The chemistry behind this involves two key players: a light-emitting molecule called *luciferin* and an enzyme called *luciferase*. When luciferin reacts with oxygen, catalyzed by luciferase, it oxidizes to form oxyluciferin. This reaction releases energy in the form of photons, creating the glow we see in fireflies, fungi, and deep-sea creatures.

(2) In the pitch-black depths of the ocean, where sunlight cannot penetrate, bioluminescence serves critical survival functions. For the Anglerfish, a glowing lure dangling from its head acts as bait to attract unsuspecting prey. Conversely, some species use light for defense. The "burglar alarm" effect occurs when a threatened organism lights up to attract a larger predator that will attack the original aggressor. Others, like certain squids, utilize "counter-illumination," emitting light from their bellies to match the faint sunlight from above, effectively erasing their silhouette to predators lurking below.

(3) The versatility of this biological mechanism is a testament to convergent evolution, having evolved independently at least 40 times across different lineages. Today, scientists are harnessing this chemistry for medical research, using bioluminescent markers to track the spread of cancer cells or viral infections within living tissues, proving that this ancient survival tool can shed light on modern medical mysteries.

contentBlocks.title: Text 1: Living Light
contentBlocks.content:
(1) Bioluminescence is one of nature’s most dazzling phenomena, a process where living organisms produce and emit light. Unlike the heat-generating light of a campfire or an incandescent bulb, bioluminescence is a form of "cold light"—nearly 100% of the energy is converted into illumination with minimal heat loss. The chemistry behind this involves two key players: a light-emitting molecule called *luciferin* and an enzyme called *luciferase*. When luciferin reacts with oxygen, catalyzed by luciferase, it oxidizes to form oxyluciferin. This reaction releases energy in the form of photons, creating the glow we see in fireflies, fungi, and deep-sea creatures.

(2) In the pitch-black depths of the ocean, where sunlight cannot penetrate, bioluminescence serves critical survival functions. For the Anglerfish, a glowing lure dangling from its head acts as bait to attract unsuspecting prey. Conversely, some species use light for defense. The "burglar alarm" effect occurs when a threatened organism lights up to attract a larger predator that will attack the original aggressor. Others, like certain squids, utilize "counter-illumination," emitting light from their bellies to match the faint sunlight from above, effectively erasing their silhouette to predators lurking below.

(3) The versatility of this biological mechanism is a testament to convergent evolution, having evolved independently at least 40 times across different lineages. Today, scientists are harnessing this chemistry for medical research, using bioluminescent markers to track the spread of cancer cells or viral infections within living tissues, proving that this ancient survival tool can shed light on modern medical mysteries.

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 1: The Vertical Horizon
contentBlock.content:
(1) As the global population hurtles toward 9 billion by 2050, the pressure on arable land is intensifying. Traditional agriculture, with its sprawling monocultures and heavy water usage, is struggling to keep pace without inflicting further ecological damage. Enter vertical farming: the practice of growing crops in stacked layers, often in controlled environments like skyscrapers or repurposed warehouses. Proponents hail it as the future of food security, a technological salvation that promises high yields with a minimal footprint.

(2) The mechanics of vertical farming—utilizing hydroponics (water-based) or aeroponics (mist-based) systems—allow for a drastic reduction in water usage, often up to 95% less than conventional farming. Furthermore, by bringing production into the heart of urban centers, "food miles" and associated transportation emissions are slashed. The controlled environment eliminates the vagaries of weather, ensuring year-round harvests free from droughts or floods.

(3) However, the vertical dream faces a formidable barrier: energy. Replacing the sun with LED lights requires a massive electrical input. While efficient for high-value, fast-growing crops like lettuce, herbs, and microgreens, the economic viability plummets when applied to calorie-dense staples like wheat, corn, or rice, which require immense amounts of light energy to produce biomass. Until renewable energy becomes cheap and ubiquitous, vertical farming may remain a niche solution for wealthy urbanites rather than a panacea for global hunger.

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 2: The Algorithmic Boss
contentBlock.content:
(1) The "Gig Economy" was sold to the world as the ultimate liberation of the workforce. No more 9-to-5 grind, no more hovering managers. Just you, your app, and the open road. Yet, for millions of rideshare drivers and delivery couriers, this freedom is an illusion masked by sophisticated coding. The human manager has not disappeared; they have simply been replaced by an "algorithmic boss"—a black box of code that tracks, ranks, and disciplines workers with ruthless efficiency.

(2) This form of management is insidious because it is invisible. Workers are nudged into working longer hours through gamified incentives and surge pricing. While the app promises freedom, the algorithm demands obedience. A driver who rejects too many routes risks "deactivation"—a euphemism for being fired—without a hearing or a human to plead their case. The data asymmetry is staggering; the platform knows everything about the worker's behavior, while the worker knows nothing about how their pay is calculated.

(3) Furthermore, the classification of these workers as "independent contractors" strips them of safety nets. They bear all the risks—vehicle maintenance, gas, health insurance—while the platform reaps the rewards of their labor. It is a precariat class created by design. To truly innovate, we must move beyond the "Uberization" of labor and establish a regulatory framework that demands algorithmic transparency and guarantees that flexibility does not come at the cost of basic human dignity.

To truly innovate, we must move beyond the "Uberization" of labor and establish a regulatory framework that demands algorithmic transparency and guarantees that flexibility does not come at the cost of basic human dignity.

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 3: The Memory Broker
contentBlock.content:
(1) Kael sat in the sterilization chair, the metal cold against his spine. In his hand, he clutched a small, silver vial. Inside swirled a wisp of pearlescent smoke—the memory of his seventh birthday. Specifically, the moment he stood in the backyard, feeling the first rain after the Great Drought. He remembered the smell of petrichor, the shock of cold droplets on his sunburnt skin, and the vibrant green of the reviving grass. It was a Grade-A organic memory, uncorrupted by neural implants.

(2) "This is a fine specimen," the Broker said, adjusting his monocle. The Broker’s eyes were flat and grey, likely replacements. "Pure joy mixed with relief. The wealthy clients in the Upper Spire pay a premium for 'Authentic Relief'. They have forgotten what rain feels like."

(3) "How much?" Kael asked, his voice cracking. He didn't want to sell it. That rain was the last time he remembered his mother smiling. But the rent for the oxygen filtration unit was due, and nostalgia didn't pay the bills.

(4) "Enough to keep your air filters running for three months," the Broker replied, extending a datapad.

(5) Kael hesitated. Once extracted, the memory would be gone. He would know *that* it happened, like reading a fact in a history book, but he would no longer *feel* it. The sensory texture, the emotion, the soul of the moment would belong to a stranger. He looked at the vial one last time, then placed it on the counter. As the credits transferred to his account, he felt a sudden hollowness in his chest, a phantom limb pain where a piece of his humanity used to be.

questions.question: Based on Text 4, the reviewer's overall assessment of the "Apex Tower" is that ....

options.label: A
options.text: it is a flawless masterpiece of modern engineering

options.label: B
options.text: it is a functional failure that should be demolished immediately

options.label: C
options.text: it is an architectural marvel that unfortunately ignores its social context and utility

options.label: D
options.text: it is a humble building that fits perfectly into the historic neighborhood

options.label: E
options.text: it is the most eco-friendly building ever constructed

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 4: Review: The Apex Tower
contentBlock.content:
(1) The newly inaugurated Apex Tower stands as a testament to modern engineering’s triumph over gravity. Soaring 120 stories above the financial district, its sleek, obsidian-like façade reflects the clouds, making the massive structure appear to dissolve into the sky. Architecturally, it is undeniably striking—a shard of black glass piercing the heavens. However, a building must be judged not just by how it looks on a postcard, but how it lives and breathes for its inhabitants and the city below. In this regard, the Apex Tower is a monumental failure.

(2) At the street level, the building is an act of aggression. There are no benches, no awnings, no greenery. The entrance is a fortress, guarded by biometric scanners and cold marble, signaling clearly that the public is not welcome. It is "hostile architecture" at its finest. Inside, the residential units are cavernous but strangely sterile. The floor-to-ceiling windows offer breathtaking views, but the layout forces furniture into awkward islands, prioritizing the vista over human habitability.

(3) The Apex Tower is a sculpture for the rich to store their wealth, not a home. It dominates the skyline but contributes nothing to the streetscape. It is a lonely monolith, beautiful in its isolation, but utterly devoid of the warmth and connectivity that make a city vibrant. It is a building to be looked at, but not one to be loved.

contentBlocks.title: Text 4: Review: The Apex Tower
contentBlocks.content:
(1) The newly inaugurated Apex Tower stands as a testament to modern engineering’s triumph over gravity. Soaring 120 stories above the financial district, its sleek, obsidian-like façade reflects the clouds, making the massive structure appear to dissolve into the sky. Architecturally, it is undeniably striking—a shard of black glass piercing the heavens. However, a building must be judged not just by how it looks on a postcard, but how it lives and breathes for its inhabitants and the city below. In this regard, the Apex Tower is a monumental failure.

(2) At the street level, the building is an act of aggression. There are no benches, no awnings, no greenery. The entrance is a fortress, guarded by biometric scanners and cold marble, signaling clearly that the public is not welcome. It is "hostile architecture" at its finest. Inside, the residential units are cavernous but strangely sterile. The floor-to-ceiling windows offer breathtaking views, but the layout forces furniture into awkward islands, prioritizing the vista over human habitability.

(3) The Apex Tower is a sculpture for the rich to store their wealth, not a home. It dominates the skyline but contributes nothing to the streetscape. It is a lonely monolith, beautiful in its isolation, but utterly devoid of the warmth and connectivity that make a city vibrant. It is a building to be looked at, but not one to be loved.

options.label: B

options.label: C
options.text: balance aesthetic ambition with human habitability and social integration

options.label: D
options.text: ignore the needs of the public in favor of the needs of the wealthy

options.label: E
options.text: use only traditional materials like brick and wood

options.label: A
options.text: advocate for the complete replacement of traditional agriculture with vertical farming

options.label: B
options.text: discuss the mechanics of hydroponic systems in detail

options.label: C
options.text: evaluate the potential of vertical farming as a solution to urban food security while acknowledging its limitations

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 1: The Vertical Horizon
contentBlock.content:
(1) As the global population hurtles toward 9 billion by 2050, the pressure on arable land is intensifying. Traditional agriculture, with its sprawling monocultures and heavy water usage, is struggling to keep pace without inflicting further ecological damage. Enter vertical farming: the practice of growing crops in stacked layers, often in controlled environments like skyscrapers or repurposed warehouses. Proponents hail it as the future of food security, a technological salvation that promises high yields with a minimal footprint.

(2) The mechanics of vertical farming—utilizing hydroponics (water-based) or aeroponics (mist-based) systems—allow for a drastic reduction in water usage, often up to 95% less than conventional farming. Furthermore, by bringing production into the heart of urban centers, "food miles" and associated transportation emissions are slashed. The controlled environment eliminates the vagaries of weather, ensuring year-round harvests free from droughts or floods.

(3) However, the vertical dream faces a formidable barrier: energy. Replacing the sun with LED lights requires a massive electrical input. While efficient for high-value, fast-growing crops like lettuce, herbs, and microgreens, the economic viability plummets when applied to calorie-dense staples like wheat, corn, or rice, which require immense amounts of light energy to produce biomass. Until renewable energy becomes cheap and ubiquitous, vertical farming may remain a niche solution for wealthy urbanites rather than a panacea for global hunger.

questions.question:
(Select the correct answers - there can be more than one)
Based on Text 1, which of the following statements accurately reflect the "process elements" of the CRISPR-Cas9 mechanism described in the second paragraph?

options.label: A
options.text: The Cas9 enzyme acts as a molecular pair of scissors that cuts the DNA strands.

options.label: B
options.text: The guide RNA (gRNA) is responsible for rebuilding the DNA sequence after it is cut.

options.label: C
options.text: The system identifies specific DNA sequences by matching them with the guide RNA.

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 1: The Double-Edged Sword of Gene Editing
contentBlock.content:
(1) The advent of CRISPR-Cas9 technology has revolutionized the field of molecular biology, offering a tool of unprecedented precision for editing the genome. Unlike its predecessors, which were often cumbersome, expensive, and imprecise, CRISPR is elegant in its simplicity. It utilizes a defense mechanism originally evolved by bacteria to fight off viral attacks. By repurposing this mechanism, scientists can now target specific DNA sequences within a living organism and cut them, allowing for the insertion, deletion, or modification of genetic material.

(2) The process begins with the design of a guide RNA (gRNA) that matches the target DNA sequence. This gRNA is attached to the Cas9 enzyme, which acts as a pair of molecular scissors. When introduced into the cell, the gRNA leads Cas9 to the precise location in the genome. Once there, Cas9 severs the double helix. The cell’s natural repair machinery then kicks in to heal the break. Scientists can exploit this repair process to introduce new genetic information, effectively "rewriting" the code of life.

(3) However, the accessibility and power of this technology have sparked intense bioethical debates. While the potential to cure genetic diseases like sickle cell anemia and cystic fibrosis is immense, the specter of "designer babies" looms large. The ability to edit the germline—sperm, eggs, and embryos—means changes are passed down to future generations. Furthermore, "off-target effects," where Cas9 cuts unintended parts of the genome, remain a significant safety hurdle. The scientific community is thus faced with a dilemma: how to harness this Promethean fire without burning the very fabric of human heredity.

questions.subject: Literasi
questions.subcategory: Gagasan Pendirian yang Relevan/Tidak Relevan dengan Isi Bacaan Argumentatif

contentBlock.title: Text 2: The Soul of the Machine
contentBlock.content:
(1) The recent explosion of Generative AI tools in the realm of visual arts has drawn a sharp line in the sand between technophiles and traditionalists. Proponents argue that AI represents the ultimate democratization of creativity, allowing anyone with an idea to manifest it visually without years of technical training. They envision a future where the barrier to entry for artistic expression is obliterated, fostering a new renaissance of digital content.

(2) Yet, this techno-optimism glosses over a fundamental truth: art is not merely the production of an aesthetically pleasing image; it is the crystallization of human experience, emotion, and intent. An AI, no matter how sophisticated its neural network, operates on probability and pattern recognition. It scrapes billions of images from the internet—often without the consent of the original creators—to generate a composite that mimics style but lacks substance. It produces a "visual output," not a "work of art."

(3) To cede the creative landscape to algorithms is to accept a culture of mediocrity and derivation. The "soul" of art lies in the artist's struggle, the intentional choices made during the process, and the historical context embedded in the work. An AI cannot feel grief, nor can it understand the nuance of social commentary. While AI can certainly serve as a powerful tool for ideation or workflow acceleration, treating it as a replacement for human creativity risks hollowing out the very thing that makes us human. We must regulate its integration to ensure it amplifies human agency rather than replacing it.

An AI, no matter how sophisticated its neural network, operates on probability and pattern recognition. It scrapes billions of images from the internet—often without the consent of the original creators—to generate a composite that mimics style but lacks substance. It produces a "visual output," not a "work of art."

(3) To cede the creative landscape to algorithms is to accept a culture of mediocrity and derivation. The "soul" of art lies in the artist's struggle, the intentional choices made during the process, and the historical context embedded in the work. An AI cannot feel grief, nor can it understand the nuance of social commentary. While AI can certainly serve as a powerful tool for ideation or workflow acceleration, treating it as a replacement for human creativity risks hollowing out the very thing that makes us human. We must regulate its integration to ensure it amplifies human agency rather than replacing it.

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 3: The Echo of the Oud
contentBlock.content:
(1) Elias sat in the corner of the dimly lit apartment in Chicago, the noise of the city traffic humming outside like a restless beast. In the corner, covered in a thin sheet of grey dust, stood his grandfather’s oud. It had been years since he touched the pear-shaped instrument, years since he left the olive groves of the Levant for the promise of the West. He had traded the complex quarter-tones of his heritage for the steady, predictable rhythm of a factory shift.

(2) "Baba, what is that?" his granddaughter, Elara, asked, pointing a small finger at the instrument. She spoke only English, her tongue clumsy with the few Arabic words Elias tried to teach her.

(3) Elias sighed, the weight of a thousand memories pressing on his chest. He stood up, his joints popping, and walked over to the corner. He wiped the dust, revealing the polished wood and the intricate rosettes carved into the soundboard. He sat down and cradled it. His fingers remembered what his mind had tried to forget. He plucked a string. A deep, resonant note filled the room, silencing the traffic outside.

(4) He began to play a *maqam*, a melody of longing and return. It was a song about a bird that flies too far from its nest and forgets how to sing. As the music swelled, he saw Elara’s eyes widen. She didn't understand the history, the war, or the migration that brought the oud here, but she felt the vibration of it in the air. For the first time in decades, Elias felt the bridge between his past and his future rebuilding, one note at a time.

questions.question: Based on Text 4, the reviewer’s opinion on the documentary "The Silent Reef" can be summarized as ....

options.label: A
options.text: a visually stunning but narratively disjointed attempt to address climate change

options.label: B
options.text: a perfect masterpiece that offers a guaranteed solution to ocean acidification

options.label: C
options.text: a boring and overly technical film that only scientists will appreciate

options.label: D
options.text: a well-intentioned film that fails because of its poor cinematography

options.label: E
options.text: a dangerous piece of propaganda that exaggerates environmental data

questions.subject: Literasi
questions.subcategory: Ketepatan Opini atas Objek Bahasan dalam Bacaan Ulasan

contentBlock.title: Text 4: Review: "The Silent Reef"
contentBlock.content:
(1) In his latest documentary "The Silent Reef," director Marcus Thorne attempts to tackle the colossal issue of ocean acidification and coral bleaching. Visually, the film is a triumph. The 4K underwater cinematography is nothing short of a visual feast, capturing the kaleidoscopic beauty of the Great Barrier Reef with a clarity that makes the subsequent scenes of dead, white graveyards all the more heartbreaking. The score, composed by Hans Zimmer’s protégé, swells and recedes like the tide, effectively manipulating the audience's emotions.

(2) However, as a piece of investigative journalism, the film falls short. Thorne spends a significant portion of the runtime focusing on individual consumer choices—plastic straws, sunscreen, and recycling. While these are important, the film frustratingly shies away from confronting the industrial elephants in the room: fossil fuel corporations and massive agricultural runoff. By placing the burden of salvation solely on the viewer, the documentary feels somewhat toothless.

(3) Furthermore, the narrative structure is slightly disjointed. It jumps from scientific interviews to emotional anecdotes without a strong connective thread. Despite these narrative stumbles, "The Silent Reef" succeeds in its primary goal: to make the invisible visible. It simplifies complex marine chemistry into digestible graphics that any layperson can understand. It is a beautiful, flawed, but necessary watch, even if it leaves the viewer asking for more systemic solutions.

contentBlock.title: Text 4: Review: "The Silent Reef"
contentBlock.content:
(1) In his latest documentary "The Silent Reef," director Marcus Thorne attempts to tackle the colossal issue of ocean acidification and coral bleaching. Visually, the film is a triumph. The 4K underwater cinematography is nothing short of a visual feast, capturing the kaleidoscopic beauty of the Great Barrier Reef with a clarity that makes the subsequent scenes of dead, white graveyards all the more heartbreaking. The score, composed by Hans Zimmer’s protégé, swells and recedes like the tide, effectively manipulating the audience's emotions.

(2) However, as a piece of investigative journalism, the film falls short. Thorne spends a significant portion of the runtime focusing on individual consumer choices—plastic straws, sunscreen, and recycling. While these are important, the film frustratingly shies away from confronting the industrial elephants in the room: fossil fuel corporations and massive agricultural runoff. By placing the burden of salvation solely on the viewer, the documentary feels somewhat toothless.

(3) Furthermore, the narrative structure is slightly disjointed. It jumps from scientific interviews to emotional anecdotes without a strong connective thread. Despite these narrative stumbles, "The Silent Reef" succeeds in its primary goal: to make the invisible visible. It simplifies complex marine chemistry into digestible graphics that any layperson can understand. It is a beautiful, flawed, but necessary watch, even if it leaves the viewer asking for more systemic solutions.

options.text: To advocate for a complete ban on genetic engineering due to unforeseen consequences.

options.label: B
options.text: To explain the technical process of gene editing while simultaneously highlighting the complexity of its moral application.

options.label: C
options.text: To compare CRISPR technology with older methods of gene splicing to show its superiority.

options.label: D
options.text: To provide a historical account of how Jennifer Doudna discovered the Cas9 protein.

options.label: E
options.text: To persuade investors to fund biotechnology research despite the risks involved.

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 1: The Double-Edged Sword of Gene Editing
contentBlock.content:
(1) The advent of CRISPR-Cas9 technology has revolutionized the field of molecular biology, offering a tool of unprecedented precision for editing the genome. Unlike its predecessors, which were often cumbersome, expensive, and imprecise, CRISPR is elegant in its simplicity. It utilizes a defense mechanism originally evolved by bacteria to fight off viral attacks. By repurposing this mechanism, scientists can now target specific DNA sequences within a living organism and cut them, allowing for the insertion, deletion, or modification of genetic material.

(2) The process begins with the design of a guide RNA (gRNA) that matches the target DNA sequence. This gRNA is attached to the Cas9 enzyme, which acts as a pair of molecular scissors. When introduced into the cell, the gRNA leads Cas9 to the precise location in the genome. Once there, Cas9 severs the double helix. The cell’s natural repair machinery then kicks in to heal the break. Scientists can exploit this repair process to introduce new genetic information, effectively "rewriting" the code of life.

(3) However, the accessibility and power of this technology have sparked intense bioethical debates. While the potential to cure genetic diseases like sickle cell anemia and cystic fibrosis is immense, the specter of "designer babies" looms large. The ability to edit the germline—sperm, eggs, and embryos—means changes are passed down to future generations. Furthermore, "off-target effects," where Cas9 cuts unintended parts of the genome, remain a significant safety hurdle. The scientific community is thus faced with a dilemma: how to harness this Promethean fire without burning the very fabric of human heredity.

questions.question: On the spectrum of cultural exchange, where Styrax argues for acknowledging nuance and Caltha highlights systemic harm, what unstated assumption does Quince's argument ("natural byproduct of our increasingly interconnected world") make that both Styrax and Caltha would likely criticize?

options.label: A
options.text: That all cultures have an equal desire to be shared with the mainstream.

options.label: B
options.text: That globalization is a negative force that should be resisted.

options.label: C
options.text: That dominant cultures are the only ones capable of creating new trends.

options.label: D
options.text: That cultural exchange occurs in a neutral "interconnected world" devoid of historical context or power dynamics.

options.label: E
options.text: That cultural appreciation is no longer possible in the modern world.

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: Text 1
contentBlock.content:
myrica posted: April 1, 2023 at 9:12 p.m.
In my view, cultural appropriation has the potential to shed light on underrepresented cultures. When facets of a culture are assimilated into mainstream society, it can aid in increasing the cultural group's exposure and visibility, promoting its preservation and continuity.

cedrus posted: April 1, 2023 at 11:35 p.m.
Understanding cultural appropriation requires recognizing the historical context and power dynamics that influence it. This issue goes beyond individual actions, but rather a product of systemic oppression and marginalization.

aronia posted: April 2, 2023 at 2:16 a.m.
By appropriating cultural elements without consent or comprehension, dominant cultures maintain a cycle of oppression, wherein they dominate and influence the portrayal of other cultures, leading to the erasure of cultural heritage and identity..

styrax posted April 2, 2023 at 9:57 a.m.
It is crucial to acknowledge that cultural appropriation is a complex issue that cannot always be viewed in simplistic terms, and there exists a subtle difference between genuine appreciation and inappropriate appropriation.

quince posted April 2, 2023 at 4:19 p.m.
Cultural appropriation is a natural byproduct of our increasingly interconnected world. Culture is a dynamic and evolving entity, and it has been happening for centuries for people to incorporate elements of other cultures into their own lives.

caltha posted April 3, 2023 at 12:37 a.m.
The problem with cultural appropriation is that it strips the culture of its significance and turns it into a mere costume for entertainment. It reinforces the idea that cultures can be plucked apart, rather than being seen as complex, dynamic, and interconnected systems.

questions.explanation: Quince's argument ("natural byproduct," "interconnected world") frames appropriation as a flat, neutral process. This directly ignores the core arguments of both Styrax and Caltha. Styrax explicitly calls for "genuine appreciation" vs. "inappropriate appropriation," implying context matters. Caltha argues it's *not* neutral, but "strips the culture of its significance" and is part of an interconnected *system* of a different kind. Quince's unstated assumption is that the "interconnected world" is a level playing field, an idea both Caltha (systemic harm) and Cedrus ("systemic oppression") would reject.

questions.question: What is the *rhetorical function* of Styrax's comment ("a complex issue... subtle difference") within the context of the entire discussion forum?

options.label: A
options.text: To definitively solve the debate by providing a final correct answer.

options.label: B
options.text: To dismiss the arguments of both the critics and the advocates as too simplistic.

options.label: C
options.text: To moderate the debate by cautioning against overly simplistic, black-and-white thinking and introducing a need for careful judgment.

options.label: D
options.text: To fully align with Myrica's view that appropriation helps preserve cultures.

options.label: E
options.text: To strongly support Aronia's claim that all appropriation is oppression.

questions.explanation:
This question requires matching the posts to a specific thesis. The thesis has two parts: "positive" and "natural".
* **Myrica** provides the "positive" aspect ("shed light on underrepresented cultures," "promoting its preservation").
* **Quince** provides the "natural" aspect ("natural byproduct," "interconnected world," "happening for centuries").
The other users (Aronia, Cedrus, Caltha) are critics who would argue *against* this thesis. Styrax is neutral. Therefore, Myrica and Quince are the best fit.

By appropriating cultural elements without consent or comprehension, dominant cultures maintain a cycle of oppression, wherein they dominate and influence the portrayal of other cultures, leading to the erasure of cultural heritage and identity..

styrax posted April 2, 2023 at 9:57 a.m.
It is crucial to acknowledge that cultural appropriation is a complex issue that cannot always be viewed in simplistic terms, and there exists a subtle difference between genuine appreciation and inappropriate appropriation.

quince posted April 2, 2023 at 4:19 p.m.
Cultural appropriation is a natural byproduct of our increasingly interconnected world. Culture is a dynamic and evolving entity, and it has been happening for centuries for people to incorporate elements of other cultures into their own lives.

caltha posted April 3, 2023 at 12:37 a.m.
The problem with cultural appropriation is that it strips the culture of its significance and turns it into a mere costume for entertainment. It reinforces the idea that cultures can be plucked apart, rather than being seen as complex, dynamic, and interconnected systems.

contentBlocks.title: Text 1
contentBlocks.content:
myrica posted: April 1, 2023 at 9:12 p.m.
In my view, cultural appropriation has the potential to shed light on underrepresented cultures. When facets of a culture are assimilated into mainstream society, it can aid in increasing the cultural group's exposure and visibility, promoting its preservation and continuity.

questions.explanation: Cedrus's argument is that appropriation is a "product of systemic oppression and marginalization," meaning it is rooted in historical power dynamics. This is fundamentally *incompatible* with Quince's argument that appropriation is a "natural byproduct" of an "interconnected world." Quince's premise ignores the very power dynamics that Cedrus places at the center of the issue.

questions.question: Categorize the following statements as True or False based on the passages.

options.text: Passage 1 states that Cognitive-behavioral therapy is the *only* method to overcome catastrophic thinking.
Passage 2 finds that catastrophizers in minimal combat were safer than non-catastrophizers who faced severe combat.
Both passages agree that catastrophizing is a predictive risk factor for serious mental health disorders.
assage 1 focuses on the *mechanism* and *daily-life examples* of the distortion, while Passage 2 focuses on its *predictive power* in a high-stress military group.

questions.subject: Literasi

contentBlock.title: text 2
contentBlock.content:
**Passage 1**
Catastrophizing, a cognitive distortion that leads to excessive worry and fear, has been recognized as a crucial issue in the development of anxiety disorders. This type of thinking involves interpreting events or situations in the worst possible way, leading to an overestimation of the threat and potential danger. Continued research shows that catastrophic thinking is a serious contributor to many other anxiety disorders.

At work, for instance, a perfectionist with catastrophizing tendencies might agonize over the smallest mistake. "They might engage in catastrophic thinking such as 'I'm going to get fired' and 'If I get fired, I won't be able to handle it," explains Dr Patrick Keelan, a psychologist and certified therapist in Alberta, Canada. At some point, the person's fears may reach a point where they are simply no longer able to function in their role.

Catastrophic thinking can also impact personal relationships and daily activities. A person with this cognitive distortion may anticipate disaster in social situations and avoid them altogether. [BLANK] In severe cases, catastrophic thinking can even lead to depression, as the person becomes consumed by negative thoughts and is unable to see positive outcomes in their life.

It is important to seek help from a mental health professional, as this type of thinking can be difficult to overcome on one's own. Therapy, such as cognitive-behavioral therapy, can help individuals identify and challenge their catastrophic thoughts and develop more balanced ways of thinking. With time and effort, it is possible to reduce the impact of catastrophic thinking and improve one's overall quality of life.
(Source: BBC with modifications)

**Passage 2**
When something really bad happens to you, how do you think about your future? Catastrophizers think, everything will now unravel, and my life will be ruined. This mindset turns out to be an enormous impediment to happiness and, even worse, it is a major risk factor for posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD).

We found this out by tracking every single one of the 79,438 U.S. Army soldiers who were deployed to Iraq or Afghanistan from 2009 to 2013. On their very first day in the Army, they took a psychological questionnaire asking them to rate how they felt about several statements related to pessimism and its most extreme form, catastrophization.

It turns out that we could have used the day-one questionnaire to predict robustly who would develop PTSD. Catastrophizers who faced severe combat stress were almost four times as likely as non-catastrophizers to get PTSD over the course of their service. But even those catastrophizers who faced minimal combat were at greater risk for PTSD than non-catastrophizers, and at all other levels of combat as well.

Combat is near the extreme of the bad events that human beings face. So what is the lesson for the rest of us, the civilian population? If you catastrophize, you will likely suffer more from bad events, and if you have the opposite, an optimistic mindset, you will likely be more resilient.
(Source: Time with modifications)

questions.explanation:
(A) False: Passage 1 states therapy "can help" and it's "difficult to overcome on one's own", not that it's the *only* way.
(B) False: Passage 2 states "even those catastrophizers who faced minimal combat were at *greater risk* for PTSD than *non-catastrophizers*", and does not make the comparison in this option.
(C) True: Passage 1 links it to anxiety disorders and depression. Passage 2 links it to PTSD.
(D) True: Passage 1 discusses *how* it works ("worst possible way," "overestimation of threat") and gives examples (work, relationships). Passage 2 focuses on the Army study and *predicting* PTSD.

questions.question: In Passage 2, the word "impediment" is used to frame catastrophizing as:

options.label: A
options.text: a permanent, unchangeable personality trait.

options.label: B
options.text: a moral failing rather than a simple cognitive habit.

options.label: C
options.text: a significant, measurable obstacle that actively blocks a desirable outcome (happiness).

options.label: D
options.text: a minor inconvenience that is easily overcome with positive thinking.

options.label: E
options.text: a symptom of PTSD rather than a predictive risk factor.

questions.explanation: An "impediment" is a barrier or obstruction. The passage states it is an "enormous impediment to happiness", meaning it actively *blocks* the state of being happy. It is also a "major risk factor", implying it's a significant, measurable obstacle, not a minor one (D) or just a symptom (E).

questions.question: How do Passage 1 and Passage 2 function together to build a comprehensive argument?

options.label: A
options.text: Passage 1 defines the problem (catastrophizing), and Passage 2 offers the definitive solution (joining the Army).

options.label: B
options.text: Passage 1 presents the theory and anecdotal evidence of the distortion, while Passage 2 provides large-scale, longitudinal statistical validation of its severe impact.

options.label: C
options.text: Passage 1 focuses on the historical causes of catastrophizing, while Passage 2 focuses only on its future effects.

options.label: D
options.text: Passage 1 and Passage 2 present contradictory findings, with Passage 1 claiming it's treatable and Passage 2 claiming it's a fixed trait.

options.label: E
options.text: Passage 1 describes catastrophizing in the civilian population, while Passage 2 proves it is exclusively a military problem.

Passage 2 then provides a massive, long-term study ("79,438 U.S. Army soldiers") that provides statistical proof ("robustly predict," "four times as likely") of the serious consequences (PTSD) that Passage 1 only generally alludes to ("anxiety disorders," "depression"). Option B best describes this theory-plus-validation relationship.

questions.question: The blank in paragraph 3 of Passage 1 occurs after "...avoid them altogether." Which sentence provides the most logical and consequential link to the *next* sentence ("In severe cases, ... can even lead to depression...")?

options.label: A
options.text: "Anxiety disorders, depression, post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) can cause catastrophic thinking."

options.label: B
options.text: "This behavior can lead to the inability to build a close relationship, thus, causing feelings of loneliness and isolation."

options.label: C
options.text: "Catastrophic thinking can be learned through experiences, such as growing up in a highly critical or fearful environment."

options.label: D
options.text: "They frequently seek reassurance from others about their worries and anxieties."

options.label: E
options.text: "They often assume the worst without fully considering all the available information or seeking additional data."

questions.explanation:
The question asks for a logical bridge.
* The *preceding* sentence is about avoiding social situations.
* The *following* sentence is about depression.
* Option B provides the perfect link: "avoiding social situations" (behavior) -> "inability to build relationships" (consequence) -> "feelings of loneliness and isolation" (emotional state) -> "depression" (severe outcome).
* Option A gets the causality backward. Option C introduces a new topic (origin). Options D and E are general descriptions, not specific consequences that link social avoidance to depression.

questions.question: Which of the following inferences are supported by *both* Passage 1 and Passage 2? (Select all that apply)

options.label: A
options.text: Catastrophizing involves an exaggeration of a situation's negative potential.

options.label: B
options.text: This cognitive distortion is limited to work and personal relationships and does not occur in other contexts.

options.label: C
options.text: An optimistic mindset, the opposite of catastrophizing, can be considered a protective factor against trauma.

options.label: D
options.text: Catastrophizing is the single, direct, and sole cause of both depression and PTSD.

We found this out by tracking every single one of the 79,438 U.S. Army soldiers who were deployed to Iraq or Afghanistan from 2009 to 2013. On their very first day in the Army, they took a psychological questionnaire asking them to rate how they felt about several statements related to pessimism and its most extreme form, catastrophization.

It turns out that we could have used the day-one questionnaire to predict robustly who would develop PTSD. Catastrophizers who faced severe combat stress were almost four times as likely as non-catastrophizers to get PTSD over the course of their service. But even those catastrophizers who faced minimal combat were at greater risk for PTSD than non-catastrophizers, and at all other levels of combat as well.

Combat is near the extreme of the bad events that human beings face. So what is the lesson for the rest of us, the civilian population? If you catastrophize, you will likely suffer more from bad events, and if you have the opposite, an optimistic mindset, you will likely be more resilient.
(Source: Time with modifications)

contentBlocks.title: text 2
contentBlocks.content:
**Passage 1**
Catastrophizing, a cognitive distortion that leads to excessive worry and fear, has been recognized as a crucial issue in the development of anxiety disorders. This type of thinking involves interpreting events or situations in the worst possible way, leading to an overestimation of the threat and potential danger. Continued research shows that catastrophic thinking is a serious contributor to many other anxiety disorders.

At work, for instance, a perfectionist with catastrophizing tendencies might agonize over the smallest mistake. "They might engage in catastrophic thinking such as 'I'm going to get fired' and 'If I get fired, I won't be able to handle it," explains Dr Patrick Keelan, a psychologist and certified therapist in Alberta, Canada. At some point, the person's fears may reach a point where they are simply no longer able to function in their role.

Catastrophic thinking can also impact personal relationships and daily activities. A person with this cognitive distortion may anticipate disaster in social situations and avoid them altogether. [BLANK] In severe cases, catastrophic thinking can even lead to depression, as the person becomes consumed by negative thoughts and is unable to see positive outcomes in their life.

It is important to seek help from a mental health professional, as this type of thinking can be difficult to overcome on one's own. Therapy, such as cognitive-behavioral therapy, can help individuals identify and challenge their catastrophic thoughts and develop more balanced ways of thinking. With time and effort, it is possible to reduce the impact of catastrophic thinking and improve one's overall quality of life.
(Source: BBC with modifications)

**Passage 2**
When something really bad happens to you, how do you think about your future? Catastrophizers think, everything will now unravel, and my life will be ruined. (Source: Time with modifications)

questions.explanation:
(A) True: Passage 1 calls it "interpreting events... in the worst possible way". Passage 2 gives the example, "everything will now unravel, and my life will be ruined."
(B) False: Passage 1 gives work/relationships as *examples*, not limits. Passage 2 uses a military context.
(C) True: Passage 1 implies this by offering therapy to create "more balanced ways of thinking". Passage 2 explicitly states, "if you have the opposite, an optimistic mindset, you will likely be more resilient."
(D) False: Both passages call it a "contributor" or a "risk factor", not a *sole cause*.

questions.question: All of the following are examples of the cognitive distortion described in the passages, EXCEPT:

options.label: A
options.text: Believing a single typo in an email will lead to being fired from your job.

options.label: B
options.text: Thinking your loved ones are certainly dead because they did not pick up their phone.

options.label: C
options.text: Concluding that your entire life is permanently ruined after failing one exam.

options.label: D
options.text: Feeling anxious about an important upcoming presentation and creating a detailed contingency plan for potential problems.

options.label: E
options.text: Assuming a small noise in the middle of the night means a murderer is in the house.

questions.explanation: Catastrophizing is an *irrational overestimation of threat*. Options A, B, C, and E all represent this. Option D, however, describes *preparedness*. While rooted in anxiety, creating a "contingency plan" is a rational, problem-solving action, not the cognitive distortion itself, which would be "If I mess up this presentation, I'll be fired, become homeless, and my life will be over."

questions.question: What is the most significant *implication* of the U.S. Army study finding that "even those catastrophizers who faced minimal combat were at greater risk for PTSD than non-catastrophizers"?

options.label: A
options.text: The study's methodology was flawed because it included soldiers who were not in combat.

options.label: B
options.text: The study proves that PTSD is primarily caused by an internal mindset, and external events are irrelevant.

options.label: C
options.text: A pre-existing catastrophizing mindset is a significant vulnerability factor that can be triggered *even by low-level stress*, independent of the trauma's objective severity.

options.label: D
options.text: Non-catastrophizers are completely immune to developing PTSD, even in severe combat.

options.label: E
options.text: The severity of combat is the only variable that truly matters for predicting PTSD.

questions.explanation: This specific finding is crucial. It isolates the variable. By showing that "minimal combat" (low external stress) still led to "greater risk" for catastrophizers, it implies that the *mindset itself* is a major vulnerability, not just the *event*. It's not that events are irrelevant (B) or that severity doesn't matter (E), but that the pre-existing mindset makes one vulnerable *even* at low levels of stress.

questions.question: How does paragraph 2 relate to the claim made in paragraph 1 of Passage 2?

options.label: A
options.text: It introduces a counter-argument that challenges the claim in paragraph 1.

options.label: B
options.text: It provides the detailed methodology and evidential support for the claim made in paragraph 1.

options.label: C
options.text: It narrows the claim from paragraph 1 to a specific, unrelated group that was not mentioned.

options.label: D
options.text: It explains the historical origin of the claim made in paragraph 1.

options.label: E
options.text: It re-defines the key terms of the claim made in paragraph 1.
